proposals.title: Harnessing Structural Disorder: Amorphous and Defective MOF-808 for Enhanced Water Harvesting and Ion Uptake Kinetics
proposals.motivation: The field of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has long prioritized perfect crystalline order for structural characterization and property optimization. However, for applications like atmospheric water harvesting and ion capture from aqueous solutions, which are diffusion-limited processes, the ideal pore environment may not be a perfectly ordered lattice. Pre-2026 work, including from the Yaghi group, has established the high performance of crystalline MOF-808 in these domains, but the potential of intentionally disordered variants remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing controlled amorphization or specific defect types into MOF-808 will create a more heterogeneous pore landscape with improved mass transport properties, leading to superior kinetics in water vapor adsorption/desorption cycles and alkali metal ion uptake from solution compared to the pristine, crystalline material.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOF-808 variants with varying degrees of crystallinity using established methods like ball-milling, chemical treatment, and modulated synthesis to introduce linker vacancies or cluster defects. These materials will be characterized by pair distribution function (PDF) analysis, solid-state NMR, and physisorption to quantify disorder. Their performance will be rigorously tested using gravimetric water adsorption cycles and batch ion uptake experiments, with kinetics modeled. An AI-driven analysis pipeline, using graph neural networks (GNNs) trained on crystalline MOF data and adapted to handle partial structural information, will correlate defect types and concentrations with dynamic performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-property relationship between engineered disorder and functional performance in diffusion-limited applications for MOFs. It will provide a new design paradigm, moving beyond perfect crystallinity, to optimize materials for real-world cyclic processes like water harvesting and environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: While defects are often considered detrimental, this proposal systematically engineers and leverages them as a primary design feature for enhanced kinetics, a significant departure from the crystalline perfection dogma in MOF chemistry. The integration of AI tools to parse amorphous structures for property prediction is also a novel methodological advance for the field.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Design of Hydrolytically Stable MOFs for Selective Liquid-Phase Ion Capture and Catalysis
proposals.motivation: While MOFs have revolutionized gas storage and separation, their application in complex liquid-phase environments—critical for water purification, resource recovery, and industrial catalysis—remains underexplored. A primary bottleneck is the scarcity of MOFs that combine high hydrolytic stability with precise functionality for targeting solutes in aqueous media. This limits their practical deployment for urgent challenges like extracting critical metals from brine or catalyzing reactions in water.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating generative AI models with high-throughput computational screening, we can identify and synthesize novel MOF architectures with organic linkers and metal nodes specifically engineered for exceptional aqueous stability and high affinity towards target ions or molecules in the liquid phase.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-model AI pipeline. First, a variational autoencoder (VAE) trained on known MOF structures will generate novel candidate linkers with built-in stability descriptors. Second, a graph neural network (GNN) model, pre-trained on datasets like the Cambridge Structural Database and quantum-chemical calculations of hydrolysis barriers, will predict the aqueous stability and binding energies of target species (e.g., Li+, Cs+, Pd2+) for these candidates. Promising AI-identified designs will be synthesized, and their performance validated through single-crystal diffraction and liquid-phase uptake/catalysis experiments, creating a closed-loop feedback system to refine the AI models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available AI-generated library of MOFs optimized for liquid-phase applications, moving beyond post-screening of gas-optimized databases. It will establish design principles for 'aqueous-ready' MOFs and demonstrate their efficacy in prototype systems for selective ion capture from complex brines or as catalysts for aqueous organic transformations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the dominant design paradigm: instead of screening existing MOF databases for gas properties and then testing stability, we use AI to generate structures from the ground up with liquid-phase stability and function as the primary, non-negotiable objectives.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Synthesis of MOFs with Cooperative Acid-Base Catalytic Sites for Tandem Dehydration-Hydrogenation
proposals.motivation: Performing multi-step catalytic reactions in a single reactor using heterogeneous catalysts is a major challenge for sustainable chemical synthesis, as it requires precise spatial organization of distinct active sites. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer unparalleled designability for positioning catalytic functionalities, but the rational design of frameworks with multiple, non-interfering, and cooperative sites remains largely empirical and synthetically daunting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using artificial intelligence to guide the reticular synthesis of multivariate MOFs, we can precisely install and spatially organize cooperative acid and base catalytic sites within a single framework, enabling efficient one-pot tandem catalytic sequences, such as the conversion of biomass-derived sugars to sugar alcohols.
proposals.method: We will employ a closed-loop, AI-driven workflow. First, a fine-tuned large language model (e.g., ChatGPT API) trained on chemical literature will propose candidate linker molecules with acid (e.g., -SO3H) and base (e.g., -NH2) functionalities compatible with Zr6-based MOF-808 topology. Molecular dynamics and DFT calculations, accelerated by graph neural networks, will predict site proximity and synergy. An automated synthesis platform, informed by these models, will execute high-throughput solvothermal reactions. The resulting crystals will be analyzed via in-situ spectroscopy and tested for catalytic performance, with data fed back to refine the AI models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, AI-accelerated methodology for the design of complex, multifunctional MOF catalysts. It will demonstrate a working catalyst for a challenging tandem reaction, providing fundamental insights into the spatial requirements for cooperative catalysis in confined porous environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fully integrated AI-driven design cycle—from computational prediction of compatible functional linkers to automated synthesis—specifically targeting non-trivial spatial arrangements of antagonistic acid and base sites within a single MOF crystal for tandem catalysis, moving beyond single-site or randomly mixed multivariate systems.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Computational Screening and Generative Design of Hydrolytically Stable MOFs for Atmospheric Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are exceptional candidates for atmospheric water harvesting, but their practical deployment is often limited by hydrolytic instability under real-world cycling conditions. Pre-2026, the discovery of new, stable MOFs relies heavily on iterative experimental synthesis, which is slow and resource-intensive. A predictive computational framework is urgently needed to accelerate the identification of optimal, robust structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating high-throughput quantum chemical calculations for stability assessment with generative deep learning models, we can predict and design novel MOF topologies with superior hydrolytic stability and water uptake capacity that are synthetically accessible.
proposals.method: We will first curate a large database of hypothetical MOFs using known building blocks and topologies. High-throughput screening will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations, specifically using the VASP software with dispersion-corrected functionals, to compute water adsorption energies and assess thermodynamic stability against hydrolysis. Concurrently, we will train a variational autoencoder (VAE) or a graph neural network (GNN) on existing MOF structures and stability data to generate novel, stable candidate structures. Promising candidates will be ranked by predicted performance metrics and synthetic feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible computational pipeline for the rapid discovery of hydrolytically stable MOFs. It will yield a ranked list of top candidate structures for experimental validation, directly guiding synthesis efforts. The generative model will provide fundamental insights into the structural and chemical features governing MOF stability under humid conditions.
proposals.novelty: While computational screening of MOFs for gas adsorption is established, the targeted prediction of hydrolytic stability for water harvesting remains a significant challenge. The novelty lies in the combined application of high-throughput stability screening with generative AI models specifically tuned for this property, moving beyond passive screening to active design of durable water-harvesting materials.

proposals.title: Accelerated Design and Prototyping of MOF-808-Based Industrial Pressure Swing Adsorption Towers for Oxygen Generation
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like MOF-808 have demonstrated exceptional gas uptake capacities and selectivities at the laboratory scale, offering transformative potential for energy-efficient industrial gas separations such as oxygen generation from air. However, a critical gap exists in translating these material properties into reliable, scalable adsorption tower designs, a process traditionally slow and reliant on empirical iteration. Bridging this scale-up gap is essential for realizing the practical, large-scale energy savings promised by MOFs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-fidelity thermodynamic adsorption data from single-crystal-level studies with multi-physics process modeling and generative AI for design optimization will enable the rapid and successful prototyping of a scalable, MOF-808-based pressure swing adsorption (PSA) system with superior performance metrics compared to conventional zeolite-based systems.
proposals.method: We will first synthesize and characterize bulk MOF-808, validating its O2/N2 adsorption isotherms and kinetics against single-crystal data. A digital twin of a multi-bed PSA system will be constructed using Aspen Adsorption software, incorporating this thermodynamic data. We will then employ a reinforcement learning (RL) agent, trained on this simulation environment, to autonomously optimize cycle parameters (pressures, step times) and tower geometry for maximal O2 purity and recovery. The AI-proposed optimal design will be fabricated and tested in a benchtop, multi-column PSA prototype.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, AI-accelerated framework for scaling MOF-based adsorption processes, moving beyond single-material characterization to integrated system design. It will produce a functional prototype demonstrating the industrial viability of MOF-808 for oxygen generation, providing critical performance and durability data. The methodology establishes a new paradigm for co-designing adsorbents and processes, significantly shortening the development timeline for next-generation separation technologies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of precise single-crystal-level MOF property data with generative AI-driven process optimization, a step beyond conventional sequential material-then-process development. This approach directly addresses the scale-up bottleneck by using AI to navigate the complex design space of a full PSA system tailored to a specific MOF's unique adsorption characteristics.

proposals.title: Predictive Design of Dynamic Covalent Linkers for Adaptive Metal-Organic Frameworks with Tunable Water Uptake
proposals.motivation: The development of porous materials with adaptive, stimuli-responsive behavior is a major frontier in materials science, with direct applications in smart water harvesting and selective ion capture. While Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) offer exceptional porosity, their static nature limits their ability to dynamically reconfigure in response to humidity or guest molecules. Dynamic covalent chemistry (DCC) provides a powerful tool for introducing adaptability, but its integration into crystalline frameworks lacks predictive design rules, especially for heterocyclic systems relevant to coordination chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the exchange kinetics and thermodynamics of dynamic covalent bonds (C=N, B–N) within aromatic ditopic linkers can be systematically predicted and tuned via substituent effects, enabling the rational synthesis of MOFs with precisely engineered adaptive porosity for water vapor.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations, using the Gaussian 16 suite with the ωB97X-D functional and def2-TZVP basis set, to model the free energy barriers and equilibrium constants for imine or boroxine exchange in a diverse library of heterocyclic cores. Machine learning models (e.g., gradient-boosted regression trees) will be trained on these DFT datasets to rapidly screen substituent effects using Hammett parameters. The top computational candidates will be synthesized as ditopic ligands and incorporated into zirconium-based MOFs (e.g., MOF-808 analogs), with their dynamic behavior and water adsorption isotherms characterized by variable-temperature NMR, in-situ PXRD, and gravimetric vapor sorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, computationally guided framework for designing dynamic covalent linkages in porous materials. It will deliver new adaptive MOFs whose pore apertures and hydrophilicity can be tuned by environmental humidity, directly advancing the field of atmospheric water harvesting. The validated structure-dynamicity correlations will provide a general toolbox for creating self-healing polymeric and framework materials.
proposals.novelty: This project moves beyond exploratory synthesis by establishing *a priori* design principles for dynamicity in crystalline frameworks, uniquely combining high-throughput DFT screening with Hammett parameter analysis to control bond exchange rates in a predictive manner for porous materials applications.

proposals.title: Metal-Free, Catalytic Transetherification for Dynamic Covalent Chemistry in Heteroaromatic Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Dynamic covalent chemistry (DCC) in reticular materials, exemplified by covalent organic frameworks (COFs) with imine linkages, enables self-healing and processability. However, the scope of DCC is largely limited to C=N bonds, restricting the chemical diversity and property tuning of adaptive frameworks. Extending DCC to robust heteroaromatic C–O and C–S bonds, inspired by vitrimer chemistry, would unlock new classes of stimuli-responsive porous materials with tunable electronic and chemical stability properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that organocatalytic transetherification, analogous to vitrimer exchange mechanisms, can be applied to heteroaromatic C–X (X = O, S) bonds under mild conditions, enabling the synthesis of dynamic covalent organic frameworks (dCOFs) with reversible bond formation and network rearrangement.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model furan- and thiophene-based molecular building blocks with pendant hydroxyl or thiol groups. Using high-throughput experimentation guided by AI-assisted reaction condition screening (leveraging prior work on AI for materials discovery), we will test a library of metal-free Lewis acidic (e.g., B(C6F5)3) and Brønsted acid catalysts for their ability to mediate reversible C–O/C–S bond exchange in solution, monitored by NMR and mass spectrometry. Successful catalytic systems will be applied to the solvothermal synthesis of crystalline 2D and 3D frameworks, with dynamic behavior characterized by variable-temperature solid-state NMR and stress-relaxation experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for metal-free, catalytic dynamic covalent chemistry beyond imines, directly leading to new families of dCOFs. It will provide fundamental insights into the kinetics and thermodynamics of heteroaromatic bond exchange, expanding the toolbox for creating adaptive porous materials with potential applications in molecular separations and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While vitrimer transetherification is known for aliphatic systems, its application to aromatic C–O/S bonds in the context of crystalline, porous organic frameworks is unexplored. This represents a strategic fusion of polymer network concepts with reticular chemistry to overcome a long-standing limitation in DCC.

proposals.title: Systematic Pore Engineering in Porous Graphene Frameworks (PGFs) for Enhanced Alkali Metal Ion Uptake and Transport
proposals.motivation: The performance of next-generation batteries and ion-harvesting systems critically depends on the design of electrode and sorbent materials with precise control over ion selectivity and transport kinetics. While Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) have been extensively studied for ion uptake, as in MOF-808, the emerging class of Porous Graphene Frameworks (PGFs) offers a complementary platform with potentially superior electrical conductivity and chemical stability. A systematic understanding of how pore geometry in PGFs dictates ion adsorption and diffusion remains a significant knowledge gap, hindering their rational design for energy applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically varying the pore size and shape in PGFs, we can establish clear structure-property relationships that govern the thermodynamics and kinetics of alkali metal ion (Li⁺, Na⁺) uptake from aqueous or organic electrolytes, leading to optimized materials for battery anodes or selective ion harvesting.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental workflow. First, a generative AI model (a fine-tuned GPT architecture) will be used to propose and screen hypothetical PGF structures with defined pore geometries (e.g., slit-like, circular, hexagonal) and sizes (5-20 Å). Promising candidates will be synthesized via bottom-up organic synthesis or controlled etching of graphene precursors, as demonstrated in prior single-crystal-level studies. The synthesized PGFs will be characterized and tested for alkali metal ion uptake using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to obtain thermodynamic parameters, and their electrochemical performance will be evaluated in half-cell configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework linking PGF pore geometry to ion uptake capacity, binding affinity, and diffusion barriers. It will deliver a curated dataset of structure-property relationships and identify optimal PGF architectures for fast-charging battery anodes or selective ion recovery from water. The integration of generative AI for material design will serve as a blueprint for accelerated discovery of porous carbons.
proposals.novelty: While pore engineering is established for MOFs, applying this paradigm to the conductive, tunable PGF platform for alkali metal ions is novel. The approach uniquely combines generative AI for structure proposal with precise synthesis and single-crystal-level thermodynamic validation, moving beyond heuristic design.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Templating for Programmable Pore Architectures in Water-Harvesting Porous Coordination Frameworks
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of atmospheric water harvesting in porous materials like MOFs is critically dependent on pore size and geometry, which dictate water uptake capacity and the relative pressure of capillary filling. Current synthesis methods for porous frameworks, such as PGFs (Porous Graphene Frameworks), often yield a distribution of pore sizes, limiting the precision needed to optimize water sorption isotherms for specific climates. Achieving atomic-level control over pore architecture remains a fundamental challenge in materials chemistry with direct implications for water security.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the co-assembly of molecular building blocks with shape-persistent supramolecular templates (e.g., crown ethers, cyclodextrins, or custom-designed hydrogen-bonded assemblies) will direct the formation of PGFs with uniform, template-defined pore dimensions and functionalities, leading to enhanced and predictable water uptake properties.
proposals.method: The approach integrates synthesis, characterization, and AI-driven analysis. We will synthesize a series of PGFs using solvothermal methods with selected organic templates of varying size and geometry. Advanced characterization (in-situ PXRD, gas/vapor sorption, single-crystal X-ray diffraction) will map the structure-property relationship. Concurrently, we will employ a ChatGPT-4-based conversational AI agent (inspired by the 'AI4X Roadmap') to analyze synthesis parameters, characterization data, and literature, generating hypotheses for optimal template-linker combinations to target specific pore metrics for water harvesting.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable supramolecular templating strategy for the rational design of pores in extended frameworks. It will deliver a new family of PGFs with engineered hydrophilicity and pore size, pushing the boundaries of materials-by-design. The integration of an interactive AI tool will create a feedback loop for accelerated discovery, contributing a novel methodology to the field of porous materials research.
proposals.novelty: While templating is known in zeolite chemistry, its application to dynamically form covalent organic frameworks or PGFs with precise, non-default pore geometries is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in using discrete, removable supramolecular entities as pore-defining agents during framework formation, coupled with an AI-augmented design cycle to decode the templating rules.

proposals.title: Surfactant-Templated Synthesis of Monolayer Porous Graphitic Frameworks for Enhanced Water Vapor Uptake
proposals.motivation: Porous graphitic frameworks (PGFs) offer exceptional chemical stability and potential for high surface areas, but their synthesis typically yields bulk, non-exfoliated materials with inaccessible pores. This limits their performance in applications like atmospheric water harvesting, where maximizing accessible surface area and optimizing pore chemistry for rapid, reversible water uptake is critical. A method to synthesize monolayer, crystalline PGFs would unlock their full potential for water sorption and other molecular capture applications.
proposals.hypothesis: That a surfactant monolayer at an air-water interface can pre-organize polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) precursors and confine their catalytic cyclodehydrogenation into a two-dimensional, covalent porous graphitic network, producing a free-standing, monolayer PGF sheet.
proposals.method: We will adapt surfactant-templated interfacial polymerization techniques from 2D polymer synthesis. A Langmuir-Blodgett trough will be used to form a compressed monolayer of amphiphilic surfactants (e.g., stearic acid) doped with PAH monomers like hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene derivatives at an air-water interface. Oxidative coupling or Scholl reaction conditions will be initiated by adding a catalyst (e.g., FeCl3) to the aqueous subphase or via vapor-phase delivery. The reaction progress will be monitored in situ with surface pressure-area isotherms and Brewster angle microscopy. The resulting floating monolayer will be transferred onto substrates for characterization by TEM, AFM, and X-ray scattering.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first synthetic route to exfoliated, monolayer PGFs, providing a new class of 2D covalent organic frameworks with graphitic walls. It will directly enable the measurement of intrinsic water vapor adsorption isotherms and kinetics on a single-layer material, revealing fundamental structure-property relationships. The developed method will provide a general platform for creating ultrathin, porous carbonaceous membranes for separation and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While interfacial synthesis is known for 2D COFs/MOFs, applying this confinement strategy to the synthesis of extended, fully sp2-carbon, porous graphitic monolayers is unprecedented. The approach innovatively combines colloidal chemistry templating with solid-state cyclodehydrogenation reactions typically used for bulk graphitic materials.

proposals.title: Kinetically Controlled Irreversible Synthesis of Crystalline Porous Graphene Frameworks as Robust Templates for Nanomaterial Encapsulation
proposals.motivation: Crystalline porous materials like MOFs and COFs have revolutionized materials science, but their reversible bond formation often limits their stability in harsh chemical environments. This restricts their use as robust, sacrificial templates for synthesizing nanomaterials that require aggressive processing conditions. A crystalline porous graphene framework (PGF) with irreversible covalent bonds would offer exceptional stability, enabling new templating pathways for creating confined nanostructures relevant to catalysis, energy storage, and separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing irreversible coupling reactions under precise kinetic control of monomer addition and reaction conditions, we can direct the crystallization of porous graphene frameworks (PGFs) with permanent, well-defined porosity and exceptional stability, making them viable as programmable templates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PGFs using kinetically controlled, irreversible Diels-Alder or nucleophilic aromatic substitution reactions between designed molecular building blocks. Reaction parameters (temperature, concentration, addition rate) will be modulated using feedback from in-situ characterization (PXRD, Raman) guided by machine learning models (e.g., Bayesian optimization) to identify conditions favoring crystallization over amorphous precipitation. The resulting crystalline PGFs will be used as templates to infiltrate metal precursors (e.g., for MOF-808 analogues) or other functional species, followed by template removal via calcination or harsh chemical etching to recover the templated nanomaterials.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general synthetic strategy for kinetically controlled crystallization of covalently robust porous frameworks. It will deliver a new class of chemically inert, crystalline templates, expanding the toolbox for nanomaterial synthesis under conditions incompatible with reversible covalent organic frameworks (COFs) or metal-organic frameworks (MOFs).
proposals.novelty: The approach merges the concept of kinetic control—common in MOF synthesis—with irreversible covalent bond formation to create crystalline graphene frameworks, a significant departure from the reversible dynamics typically used to achieve crystallinity in porous organic materials.

proposals.title: Guest-Stabilized Metal-Organic Frameworks: A Computational and Synthetic Strategy for Designing MOFs with Guest-Dependent Structural Integrity
proposals.motivation: The structural stability of most MOFs is compromised by guest removal, leading to framework collapse and limiting their application in cyclic processes like water harvesting or gas storage. Designing MOFs where guest molecules are integral to structural integrity, rather than a liability, would create a new class of materials with intrinsic resilience and function tied to their loaded state. This addresses a fundamental challenge in materials science: creating porous solids whose stability is not a static property but a dynamic, guest-responsive one.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically designing metal-organic frameworks with coordination geometries and ligand flexibilities that require guest molecules to complete their local coordination environment or lock in a rigid, porous architecture, we can create MOFs that are thermodynamically stable only when occupied by specific guests.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual computational and synthetic approach. First, we will use generative AI models, specifically variational autoencoders (VAEs) and graph neural networks (GNNs) trained on pre-2026 databases (e.g., CSD, hypothetical MOF libraries), to design linker and node combinations where the predicted lowest-energy structure requires a guest to satisfy metal coordination or prevent ligand entanglement. Second, guided by these designs, we will synthesize targeted Zr- and Al-based MOFs (e.g., derivatives of MOF-808) with pendent donor sites or tailored cavities. Stability will be assessed at the single-crystal level using in-situ X-ray diffraction and thermogravimetric analysis during guest adsorption/desorption cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for 'guest-stabilized' porous materials, moving beyond guest-tolerance to guest-necessity. It will deliver a small library of validated MOFs whose stability is explicitly coupled to their function, such as water harvesting, where the material remains robust only while hydrated. The AI-generated design rules will provide a roadmap for creating the next generation of responsive, durable sorbents.
proposals.novelty: While guest-induced structural transformations (breathing) are known, the intentional design of MOFs that are fundamentally unstable as empty frameworks and require a guest for their basic structural integrity is a non-obvious inversion of traditional MOF design paradigms. The integration of generative AI to specifically target this guest-dependent stability criterion is a novel methodological advance.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Synthesis of MOF-Polymer Composites for Enhanced Atmospheric Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like MOF-303 and MOF-808 have demonstrated exceptional atmospheric water harvesting (AWH) capacity, but their practical deployment is hindered by stability and processability issues. Integrating MOFs with polymers can enhance mechanical robustness and manage water uptake/release kinetics, yet the vast design space for MOF-polymer composites remains largely unexplored through traditional trial-and-error methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an artificial intelligence (AI) model trained on existing MOF and polymer property data can predict optimal composite formulations that synergistically combine high water uptake (from the MOF) with enhanced kinetics and stability (from the polymer), leading to superior AWH performance.
proposals.method: We will employ a closed-loop, AI-guided synthesis and testing platform. A graph neural network (GNN) model, pre-trained on databases like the Cambridge Structural Database and polymer property datasets, will be fine-tuned with in-house data on MOF-808 and MOF-303 composites. The model will propose candidate composites, which will be synthesized via in-situ polymerization or infiltration methods. Their AWH performance will be characterized at the single-crystal and bulk levels using microbalance and breakthrough column techniques. The resulting data will be fed back to iteratively refine the AI model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, AI-accelerated framework for designing functional MOF composites. It will yield new, high-performance composite materials for water harvesting with validated stability and uptake profiles. Furthermore, it will generate a curated dataset linking composite composition to AWH thermodynamics and kinetics, a valuable resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While AI has been used to screen pure MOFs, its application to guide the synthesis of MOF-polymer composites for a targeted function like water harvesting is novel. The approach moves beyond predicting static properties to actively guiding the creation of new, multi-component materials with emergent properties.

proposals.title: Bioinspired Selective Gating in Metal-Organic Frameworks: Mimicking Ion Channel Proteins for High-Fidelity Molecular Separations
proposals.motivation: The separation of molecules with similar sizes and properties, such as ethylene/ethane or xenon/krypton, remains a major energy-intensive challenge in the chemical industry. Current porous materials, including many MOFs, often rely on size-exclusion or equilibrium binding, which can be insufficient for these critical separations. This project addresses the need for next-generation separation materials that achieve kinetic selectivity through precise pore-wall engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: By deliberately functionalizing the pores of a robust, isoreticular MOF platform (e.g., MOF-808 or UiO-66) with chemical groups that mimic the selectivity filter of biological ion channels, we can create a synthetic 'gate' that preferentially allows diffusion of one target molecule over a similarly sized competitor based on differential stabilization of diffusion transition states.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of functionalized variants of a chosen Zr-MOF platform, installing precise chemical functionalities (e.g., carboxylates, amides, crown ethers) at defined positions along the pore walls via pre- and post-synthetic modification. High-throughput computational screening using Grand Canonical Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulations, guided by AI models trained on known MOF structures and gas adsorption data, will identify promising functional group/target molecule pairs. The synthesized MOFs will be characterized and their separation performance for target gas pairs (e.g., C2H4/C2H6) will be evaluated using single-component and mixed-gas diffusion measurements, transient breakthrough experiments, and in-situ spectroscopy to probe binding dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for MOFs that moves beyond equilibrium adsorption selectivity to achieve kinetic selectivity via bioinspired gating. It will deliver a family of advanced separation materials with validated performance for challenging gas pairs. Furthermore, it will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between pore-wall chemistry, diffusion barriers, and separation fidelity in confined environments.
proposals.novelty: While MOF functionalization for enhanced binding is common, the explicit design of a pore to act as a *kinetic gate* inspired by the mechanistic action of ion channel proteins is a novel paradigm. This approach focuses on controlling the diffusion pathway itself, rather than just the equilibrium affinity, to solve separations where size exclusion fails.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Screening and Single-Crystal Validation of MOF-808 Derivatives for Selective Alkali Metal Ion Separation from Aqueous Brines
proposals.motivation: The efficient separation of alkali metal ions (e.g., Li⁺, Na⁺, K⁺) from aqueous sources is critical for resource recovery, water purification, and battery recycling. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like MOF-808 offer tunable pores and high stability, but predicting and validating their selectivity for specific ions in complex aqueous mixtures remains a significant challenge. This work addresses the need for precise, energy-efficient separation materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic functionalization of the MOF-808 node, guided by AI-predicted binding thermodynamics, will yield specific derivatives with exceptional selectivity for target alkali metal ions (e.g., Li⁺ over Na⁺) in aqueous solution, which can be directly measured at the single-crystal level.
proposals.method: We will employ a ChatGPT-4/Code Interpreter-augmented workflow to generate and screen a library of MOF-808 node functional groups based on pre-2026 DFT databases. The AI will predict thermodynamic parameters (ΔG, ΔH) for ion uptake. Promising candidates (e.g., -SO₃H, -PO₃H₂ modifications) will be synthesized. Selectivity will be rigorously quantified using single-crystal X-ray diffraction and in-situ micro-gravimetric analysis on individual crystals exposed to mixed-ion solutions, directly linking structure to function.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a closed-loop, AI-informed experimental pipeline for targeted MOF design for ion separation. It will provide unprecedented, direct single-crystal-level data on competitive ion uptake in water, moving beyond powder averages. The resulting structure-selectivity relationships will serve as a blueprint for designing next-generation separation materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of generative AI for hypothesis generation in MOF chemistry with definitive experimental validation at the single-crystal level, a precision rarely applied to aqueous ion separation studies. This moves beyond bulk adsorption measurements to reveal the fundamental atomic-scale mechanisms of selectivity.

proposals.title: Zeolite-Inspired Morphological Control of Metal-Organic Frameworks for Enhanced Water Uptake Kinetics
proposals.motivation: The performance of MOFs in applications like atmospheric water harvesting is critically dependent on crystal morphology, which influences diffusion pathways and uptake kinetics. Current MOF synthesis often yields polydisperse particles with uncontrolled shapes, limiting mass transport efficiency. Zeolite science has long mastered morphological control using structure-directing agents (SDAs), a strategy largely untapped for MOFs.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing zeolite-like organic SDAs and hydrothermal synthesis conditions, we can direct the crystallization of prototypical water-harvesting MOFs (e.g., MOF-303, MOF-801) into specific, uniform morphologies (e.g., thin plates, hollow structures) that enhance water vapor diffusion and uptake rates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of cationic, multi-ammonium organic molecules as SDAs, inspired by zeolite templating agents. These will be introduced into standard MOF-303 and MOF-801 synthesis gels. Reactions will be conducted under hydrothermal/solvothermal conditions, systematically varying SDA concentration, temperature, and time. The resulting crystals will be characterized by SEM, PXRD, and gas/vapor sorption analysis. AI-assisted image analysis (using a pre-trained convolutional neural network) will quantify morphological distributions and correlate them with kinetic data from dynamic vapor sorption measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for controlling MOF crystal habit, directly translating a powerful zeolite synthesis concept to the MOF field. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships linking engineered morphology to enhanced water uptake kinetics, leading to superior performing materials for atmospheric water harvesting devices.
proposals.novelty: While SDAs are foundational in zeolite chemistry, their deliberate use to control the external morphology (not just the internal pore structure) of MOFs is a novel and non-obvious synthesis strategy. Combining this with AI-driven morphological analysis for performance prediction represents a unique, data-informed materials design approach.

proposals.title: Atomic-Level Mapping of Reversible Guest-Induced Structural Dynamics in Flexible MOFs via In Situ Single-Crystal X-ray Diffraction and AI-Enhanced Analysis
proposals.motivation: Understanding the atomic-level structural transformations in flexible Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) during gas adsorption is crucial for designing next-generation materials for gas storage, separation, and sensing. While the phenomenon of breathing or gate-opening is known, the precise, reversible pathways of bond rotation, ligand displacement, and metal-node distortion remain poorly resolved at the single-crystal level. This knowledge gap limits our ability to rationally engineer MOFs with tailored responsiveness for applications like water harvesting and selective ion uptake.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reversible single-crystal-to-single-crystal (SCSC) transformations in selected flexible MOFs (e.g., MIL-53, DUT-8) proceed via well-defined, cooperative atomic motions that can be directly visualized and quantified through in situ diffraction. We further posit that these transformations follow distinct, predictable energetic pathways dependent on guest molecule identity and pressure.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-quality single crystals of model flexible MOFs. In situ single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXRD) data will be collected across controlled pressure isotherms of key gases (e.g., CO2, H2O, N2) using a gas-cell apparatus. The resulting time-resolved structural datasets will be analyzed using a hybrid approach: classical refinement will provide initial models, followed by application of machine learning algorithms (specifically, graph neural networks trained on known MOF structures) to identify latent patterns in atomic displacement parameters and symmetry changes. This AI4X-inspired analysis will map the transformation pathways and correlate structural metrics with adsorption thermodynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomic-level, dynamic 'movie' of reversible framework flexibility in MOFs, directly linking guest uptake to specific structural coordinates. It will establish a rigorous experimental and computational methodology for studying SCSC transformations. The findings will yield design principles for creating MOFs with pre-programmed structural responses, advancing their utility in energy-efficient separations and atmospheric water harvesting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, atomic-resolution correlation of adsorption isotherms with crystallographically refined structural changes within a *single* crystal, avoiding averaging effects from polycrystalline studies. Furthermore, integrating AI-driven analysis of diffraction data to decode complex, cooperative atomic motions represents a significant methodological advance over traditional refinement.

proposals.title: Programmable Dynamic Covalent Chemistry in Metal-Organic Frameworks for Adaptive Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: The stability of MOFs is traditionally defined by strong, static coordination bonds. However, biological systems achieve complex functions through dynamic, reversible interactions. In water harvesting, the ability to dynamically reconfigure pore chemistry or structure in response to humidity changes could optimize uptake and release kinetics, a key challenge for atmospheric water harvesting devices. This research re-conceptualizes framework lability from a flaw into a programmable feature for next-generation functional materials.
proposals.hypothesis: By intentionally incorporating metal nodes and linker functionalities that undergo reversible coordination under ambient humidity cycles, we can synthesize MOFs whose labile bonds enable adaptive pore environments, leading to enhanced water uptake capacity and lower regeneration energy compared to static frameworks.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOFs using metals with known dynamic coordination behavior (e.g., Zn²⁺, Co²⁺) paired with carboxylate linkers containing secondary donor sites (e.g., pyridyl, imidazole). Water adsorption isotherms and in-situ variable-humidity PXRD/IR will characterize the dynamic structural response. Guided by the 'AI4X' paradigm, we will employ a GPT-based language model fine-tuned on the Cambridge Structural Database and reaction literature to predict promising metal-linker combinations and synthetic conditions that favor reversible bond breaking, accelerating the discovery of 'dynamic covalent' MOFs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for 'dynamic covalent MOFs' (DC-MOFs), explicitly leveraging labile bonds for function. It will deliver a materials platform with tunable adsorption kinetics for water harvesting. Furthermore, it will provide a generalizable AI-assisted workflow for predicting and validating dynamic behavior in porous crystals, moving beyond static structure prediction.
proposals.novelty: While MOF instability in water is well-documented, the deliberate design of frameworks where reversible bond dissociation is integral to the material's function—akin to a molecular machine—is a radical departure. The integration of AI to guide the synthesis of such metastable, functional states is also unprecedented in porous materials science.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Synthesis of Isoreticular MOFs: Mapping Linker Design to Porosity, Stability, and Water Uptake
proposals.motivation: The ability to rationally design Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) for targeted applications like atmospheric water harvesting is limited by empirical correlations between linker structure and material properties. A systematic, predictive understanding of how organic linker length and functionality govern key metrics—such as pore size distribution, hydrolytic stability, and adsorption isotherm shape—is critically needed to accelerate the discovery of next-generation sorbents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically varying the length and functional groups (e.g., -NH2, -OH, -CF3) of linear dicarboxylate linkers within an isoreticular MOF series (e.g., based on the MOF-808 or UiO-66 topology), we can establish quantitative structure-property relationships (QSPRs) that predict water uptake capacity, working capacity, and stability under cyclic conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize an isoreticular series of Zr-based MOFs using linkers of systematically increasing length (e.g., from benzene-1,4-dicarboxylate to biphenyl-4,4'-dicarboxylate and beyond) and varied functionalities. High-throughput characterization (PXRD, N2 porosimetry, water sorption) will generate a robust dataset. This data will train a machine learning model (e.g., a graph neural network or random forest regressor) to map linker molecular descriptors (e.g., length, functional group Hammett parameters, topological surface area) to the measured material properties, enabling predictive design.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear, quantitative design map for tuning MOF properties via linker engineering, directly informing the synthesis of optimized water-harvesting materials. It will deliver a predictive AI model that reduces the experimental burden for screening new linkers and provides fundamental insights into the interplay between hydrophilicity, pore size, and framework stability.
proposals.novelty: While isoreticular chemistry is well-established, the systematic integration of high-throughput experimental data with AI to decode and predict the multi-variable impact of linker design on *water* sorption performance—specifically for atmospheric harvesting—represents a novel, targeted approach beyond traditional gas adsorption studies.

proposals.title: Decoupling Pore Hierarchy in MOFs: Assessing the Sufficiency of Single-Pore-Type Architectures for Integrated Water Capture and Catalytic Conversion
proposals.motivation: Hierarchically porous MOFs, integrating micro-, meso-, and macropores, are widely pursued for integrated capture and conversion applications, such as atmospheric water harvesting followed by catalytic splitting. The synthesis of such complex architectures is challenging and may not be necessary if a single, optimally sized pore regime can simultaneously facilitate rapid mass transport and provide sufficient active sites. This work challenges a prevailing design paradigm to potentially simplify material synthesis for scalable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed MOF with only a mesoporous or macroporous structure, but with precisely tuned surface chemistry and active site density, can achieve performance metrics for integrated water capture and conversion comparable to those of hierarchically porous benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular MOF-808 analogs with systematically enlarged pores using extended carboxylate linkers and modulator-controlled crystallization to create distinct families of purely mesoporous (2-50 nm) and macroporous (>50 nm) single crystals. Their water adsorption isotherms and uptake kinetics will be characterized gravimetrically. Subsequently, we will functionalize these materials with identical catalytic clusters (e.g., Pt nanoparticles for water splitting) and evaluate their integrated performance in a custom apparatus that cycles between humidity-controlled capture and light-driven conversion phases. Performance will be compared to a state-of-the-art hierarchically porous Zr-MOF control.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a foundational understanding of the critical pore size requirements for dual-function capture-conversion materials, moving beyond the assumption that hierarchy is always essential. It will establish design rules for simplifying MOF architectures, potentially lowering synthesis barriers for real-world applications in water harvesting and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical porosity is a common goal, systematically testing its necessity by isolating and evaluating single-pore-type systems in a controlled, isoreticular series for integrated capture-conversion is a novel and disruptive approach. It directly questions a core materials design principle using Yaghi's established MOF platform.

proposals.title: Harnessing Linker Flexibility and Hydrogen Bonding for the Predictive Design of Functional Porous Phases in MOFs
proposals.motivation: In the synthesis of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), linker flexibility is often viewed as a source of unpredictability, leading to unwanted, non-porous, or collapsed phases that hinder targeted property design. This challenge limits the rational development of MOFs for critical applications like water harvesting and ion uptake, where specific pore geometries and chemical environments are essential. A paradigm shift is needed to transform flexibility from a liability into a design feature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically incorporating hydrogen-bonding functional groups onto flexible organic linkers, we can direct the assembly of MOFs into desired, functional porous phases through predictable, cooperative non-covalent interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental workflow. First, we will use generative AI models (VAEs/GANs) trained on pre-2026 structural databases (CSD, hypothetical MOF libraries) to design flexible linker backbones decorated with hydrogen-bonding motifs (e.g., -OH, -NH2, -COOH). Molecular dynamics and DFT calculations will simulate the free energy landscape of assembly, predicting stable porous phases. These designs will be synthesized using solvothermal methods, with structures characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction (as in recent 'Water Harvesting at the Single-Crystal Level' work) to validate the predicted phases. Functionality will be tested via water vapor and alkali metal ion uptake measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for controlling framework assembly, moving beyond rigid-linker paradigms. It will yield a family of MOFs where flexibility is functionally encoded, directly impacting the design of next-generation materials for atmospheric water harvesting and selective ion capture. The integration of AI-driven design with precise synthesis and characterization will provide a validated roadmap for predictable assembly of flexible frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often sought to minimize flexibility, this proposal actively exploits it as a design element, uniquely coupling it with hydrogen-bonding guidance for phase control. The use of generative AI to navigate the conformational space of flexible linkers for targeted porous structure prediction is a novel, non-obvious application of pre-2026 AI4Science concepts to this specific MOF challenge.

proposals.title: Decoding Local Dynamics in Polycrystalline and Amorphous Metal-Organic Frameworks via Multimodal AI-Enhanced Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The study of dynamic phenomena in MOFs, such as linker conformation changes and framework breathing, is crucial for applications in water harvesting and gas uptake but has been largely restricted to single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Most functional materials, however, exist as polycrystalline powders or amorphous phases, where conventional crystallographic methods fail. This creates a critical gap between understanding idealized structures and real-world, processable materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating multimodal spectroscopic data with physics-informed machine learning models, we can reconstruct local conformational states and quantify dynamic breathing behavior in polycrystalline and amorphous MOF samples without requiring single-crystal transformations.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of well-known breathing MOFs (e.g., MIL-53, DUT-8) in controlled polycrystalline and amorphized states. A multimodal dataset will be generated using in-situ pair distribution function (PDF) analysis from high-energy X-ray scattering, solid-state NMR, and Raman spectroscopy under varying humidity and gas pressure. A graph neural network (GNN) architecture, trained on simulated spectra from known crystal structures, will be used to map the experimental spectroscopic signatures to local atomic environments and linker conformations. A separate temporal convolutional network will analyze the time-series data to model the kinetics of framework breathing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, AI-augmented analytical framework for probing local structure and dynamics in non-crystalline porous materials. It will directly enable the study of MOF performance in realistic, polycrystalline forms for water harvesting and gas uptake, bridging a major characterization gap. The resulting open-source models will provide the community with tools to move beyond single-crystal dependency.
proposals.novelty: While AI is used for crystal structure prediction, its application to deconvolute real-time, local dynamics in amorphous and polycrystalline MOFs from multimodal spectroscopy is unprecedented. This approach relaxes the single-crystal constraint by directly linking ensemble-averaged spectroscopic data to atomic-scale motion through learned representations.

proposals.title: Urea-Functionalized MOFs as Tunable and Recyclable Heterogeneous Organocatalysts for C–C Bond Formation
proposals.motivation: Organocatalysis using small molecules like ureas is powerful for sustainable synthesis but suffers from catalyst recovery and recyclability issues. Heterogenizing these catalysts within robust, porous frameworks could overcome these limitations while enabling precise tuning of the catalytic environment. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), with their unparalleled synthetic modularity, offer an ideal platform to create solid-phase urea catalysts with enhanced activity and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating urea moieties as integral structural linkers within crystalline MOFs will create stable, porous heterogeneous organocatalysts whose activity and selectivity can be systematically tuned by varying the metal node and urea linker geometry.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Zr-based MOFs (e.g., MOF-808, UiO-66/67 analogues) using custom-designed ditopic and tritopic urea-carboxylate linkers via solvothermal methods. Catalytic performance will be tested in benchmark reactions such as nitro-Michael additions and Henry reactions, comparing activity, substrate scope, and recyclability against homogeneous urea analogues. Advanced characterization (PXRD, N2 sorption, in-situ IR, NMR) will correlate framework structure, urea accessibility, and catalytic metrics. AI-assisted analysis of synthesis parameters and performance data, guided by the AI4X roadmap, will identify optimal structure-property relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of MOF-based heterogeneous organocatalysts, demonstrating how framework confinement and order can enhance organocatalytic performance. It will provide a general design principle for immobilizing molecular catalysts within MOFs, contributing to the fields of green chemistry and advanced functional materials.
proposals.novelty: While MOFs have been used to host catalytic metal sites, their direct use as crystalline platforms for *heterogeneous* organocatalysis, where the organic catalyst is the framework linker itself, remains underexplored. This approach moves beyond simple impregnation, creating catalysts with atomic-level precision in active site placement.

proposals.title: Mapping the Structural Dynamics of MOFs: A Single-Crystal Study of Guest Exchange with Solvents and Gases
proposals.motivation: Understanding the structural transformations of Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) during guest exchange is fundamental to their application in gas storage, separation, and catalysis. Prior work, including Yaghi's studies on DMF and CS2 exchange, has established the power of single-crystal-to-single-crystal (SCSC) transformations for elucidating these mechanisms. However, a systematic investigation across a broader chemical space of guests—including industrially relevant solvents and greenhouse gases—is lacking, limiting our predictive design of robust, dynamic MOFs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the kinetics and structural pathways of guest exchange in prototypical MOFs (e.g., MOF-808, ZIF-8) are governed by specific, predictable interactions between the guest molecule's chemical functionality (polarity, size, coordination ability) and the MOF's metal node and organic linker, which can be mapped and modeled.
proposals.method: We will employ in-situ single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXRD) to monitor SCSC transformations in real-time as selected MOF crystals are exposed to controlled flows of various guest molecules. The guest library will include polar/aprotic solvents (e.g., acetone, tetrahydrofuran), alcohols, and small gases (e.g., CO2, CH4). We will use a fine-tuned large language model (e.g., a domain-adapted version of ChatGPT) to analyze and correlate the vast crystallographic datasets, extracting patterns between guest properties (from pre-2026 databases) and observed lattice parameters, bond lengths, and diffusion pathways. This AI-assisted analysis will identify key descriptors governing exchange dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, atomically resolved map of guest-exchange-induced structural dynamics across a diverse set of molecules. It will establish design rules for predicting MOF stability and selectivity during adsorption/desorption cycles. The generated dataset and AI-correlation model will serve as a foundational resource for the computational screening and synthesis of next-generation adaptive porous materials.
proposals.novelty: While SCSC studies exist for isolated guest pairs, this systematic, high-throughput crystallographic screening across multiple chemical classes of guests, coupled with AI-driven pattern recognition, is a novel approach to decode the 'chemical language' of MOF-guest interactions.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Composites of Urea-Functionalized MOFs and Hydrophilic Polymers for Enhanced Atmospheric Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: Urea-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) exhibit exceptional water uptake capacity and cooperative binding due to their flexible, hydrogen-bonding networks, as demonstrated in single-crystal studies. However, their practical deployment in atmospheric water harvesting (AWH) is limited by challenges in processability, stability under cyclic loading, and integration into devices. Creating composites with functional polymers could address these limitations while leveraging the unique thermodynamics of urea-MOFs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating urea-MOFs (e.g., MOF-801 or UiO-66-NHCONH2) into a hydrophilic polymer matrix (e.g., poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) or chitosan) will yield hierarchical composites that synergistically combine high water uptake capacity with enhanced kinetics, mechanical robustness, and efficient water release.
proposals.method: We will synthesize urea-functionalized MOFs via post-synthetic modification of Zr-based frameworks. Composites will be fabricated by in-situ MOF growth within polymer hydrogels or by blending MOF crystals with polymer solutions to form mixed-matrix membranes. Water adsorption isotherms and kinetics will be measured gravimetrically. The structure-property relationship will be elucidated using in-situ PXRD, IR spectroscopy, and electron microscopy. AI-assisted analysis (using graph neural networks on crystal structure data and random forest models on adsorption data) will help identify optimal MOF-polymer pairings and composite architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of smart, composite sorbents for AWH with tunable water uptake and release profiles. It will provide fundamental insights into how polymer environments modulate the flexible, hydrogen-bonding dynamics of urea-MOFs. The developed composites will represent a significant step toward practical, high-performance water harvesting devices.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer composites exist, none have specifically targeted the unique cooperative water binding of urea-MOFs. The novelty lies in deliberately engineering the composite interface to preserve and potentially enhance the dynamic, hydrogen-bond-driven water uptake mechanism intrinsic to these flexible frameworks.

proposals.title: Programmable Water Harvesting in Genetically Engineered Protein Crystalline Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have demonstrated exceptional performance in atmospheric water harvesting, but their synthesis often relies on petrochemical linkers and energy-intensive processes. Biological macromolecules like proteins offer inherently biocompatible, sustainable, and atomically precise building blocks, with functional side chains that could enable superior water uptake and release kinetics. This project seeks to establish a new paradigm for green, high-performance water-harvesting materials by moving from synthetic coordination chemistry to programmable biological assembly.
proposals.hypothesis: That genetically engineered protein crystals, designed with precise pore geometries and hydrophilic residue patterning, can match or exceed the water adsorption capacity and cycling stability of state-of-the-art synthetic MOFs like MOF-303, while offering lower regeneration energy.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational design pipeline using AlphaFold2 and Rosetta to model and screen protein mutants (e.g., from ferritin or lumazine synthase families) for stable crystallization and optimal pore environments. High-throughput synthesis of selected designs will be conducted via bacterial expression. Water adsorption isotherms and thermodynamics will be measured at the single-crystal level using microgravimetric analysis (QCM) and in-situ X-ray diffraction, directly comparing performance to benchmark MOFs like MOF-303 and MOF-808.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework for evaluating protein crystalline frameworks as next-generation water-harvesting materials, providing adsorption isotherms, enthalpies, and cycling data. It will deliver a new class of genetically programmable, sustainable sorbents and expand the conceptual toolkit of reticular chemistry into the biological domain.
proposals.novelty: While biomineralization and protein-MOF composites exist, using a purely protein-based crystalline framework as the primary, designed water-harvesting scaffold is unexplored. The integration of AI-driven protein design with the rigorous thermodynamic evaluation standard in MOF research creates a novel, disruptive synthesis of biology and reticular chemistry.

proposals.title: Catalytic Hydrogen Spillover in Metal-Organic Frameworks: A Combined Synthesis, Thermodynamic, and AI-Driven Investigation for Enhanced Storage
proposals.motivation: The practical implementation of hydrogen as a clean energy vector is critically limited by the need for materials that can store it at near-ambient conditions with high volumetric and gravimetric density. While MOFs offer exceptional surface areas, their hydrogen uptake via physisorption is insufficient for many applications at moderate pressures and temperatures. Hydrogen spillover, where a catalytic metal dissociates H2 and atomic hydrogen migrates onto a receptor material, is a promising but poorly understood and often irreproducible phenomenon in MOFs, representing a key bottleneck for next-generation storage materials.
proposals.hypothesis: That the strategic incorporation of well-defined, nanoscale catalytic metal clusters (e.g., Pt, Pd, Ni) into specific secondary building unit (SBU) sites of high-valency MOFs (such as MOF-808) will create efficient pathways for hydrogen dissociation and subsequent atomic hydrogen spillover into the MOF pore volume, leading to significantly enhanced hydrogen uptake capacities at 298 K and pressures below 100 bar.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOF-808 analogues with controlled loading of catalytic metal nanoparticles via post-synthetic modification and direct synthesis. Hydrogen uptake isotherms will be measured with high-precision volumetric and gravimetric systems to establish thermodynamic parameters. Advanced characterization (in-situ DRIFTS, XAS, neutron scattering) will probe the spillover mechanism. Crucially, we will employ a ChatGPT-4/Code Interpreter-augmented workflow to analyze the multi-modal dataset (synthesis conditions, structural parameters, uptake data, spectroscopic signatures), train predictive machine learning models (e.g., gradient-boosted trees, graph neural networks on MOF structures), and identify key descriptors governing efficient spillover.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative framework for understanding hydrogen spillover in MOFs, moving beyond anecdotal reports to establish design principles. It will deliver new, synthetically controlled composite materials with benchmark hydrogen storage performance at ambient temperature. Furthermore, it will demonstrate a closed-loop AI-driven materials discovery pipeline, from synthesis to prediction, specifically for complex catalytic-adsorptive phenomena.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of precise MOF synthesis, thermodynamic analysis akin to recent ion-uptake studies, and an AI-guided feedback loop to decode and optimize the spillover process, a strategy not yet applied to this long-standing challenge. This moves from simply observing spillover to actively engineering and predicting it within a robust chemical framework.

proposals.title: Mapping Hydrogen Binding Landscapes in MOF-5 and Isoreticular Frameworks via Inelastic Neutron Scattering and Computational Validation
proposals.motivation: The precise nature and energy of hydrogen binding sites in prototypical MOFs like MOF-5 are foundational to understanding gas storage, separation, and catalysis, yet remain incompletely characterized. A comprehensive, experimental map of these sites is critical for validating computational models and rationally designing next-generation materials with optimized host-guest interactions, particularly for hydrogen storage and water harvesting applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inelastic neutron scattering (INS) will reveal a discrete set of hydrogen binding sites in MOF-5 with quantifiable energies, and that systematic variation of the organic linker in isoreticular frameworks will produce predictable shifts in this binding landscape, governed by changes in pore chemistry and electrostatic potential.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-quality, deuterated single crystals of MOF-5 and its isoreticular analogues (e.g., IRMOF-3, -8). INS spectra will be collected on instruments like VISION at ORNL or TOSCA at ISIS, providing a direct probe of hydrogen vibrational modes. The experimental INS data will be analyzed and assigned using density functional theory (DFT) calculations (e.g., with VASP or CP2K) to map spectral features to specific binding sites and energies, creating a validated computational model of the host-guest interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete, experimentally validated atlas of hydrogen binding sites in the MOF-5 family, serving as a benchmark for molecular simulation force fields. It will establish a direct structure-property relationship between linker functionalization and binding site energetics, offering a concrete design principle for tuning adsorption properties in porous crystals.
proposals.novelty: While INS has been used for MOFs, a systematic, single-crystal-level study correlating INS spectra with DFT across an isoreticular series to deconvolute and energetically rank all hydrogen binding sites represents a novel and precise approach to characterizing the fundamental adsorption landscape.

proposals.title: Systematic Synthesis and Water Harvesting Performance of Mixed-Metal Zn/In Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks
proposals.motivation: The discovery of ZIF-5 (Zn/In) demonstrated that mixed-metal ZIFs can adopt topologies inaccessible to their single-metal counterparts, suggesting a vast unexplored chemical space. Pre-2026, the systematic exploration of this space for functional properties, particularly water uptake and release, remains limited. Given the established importance of MOFs for atmospheric water harvesting, developing new mixed-metal ZIFs could unlock materials with superior hydrophilicity, stability, and sorption kinetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted combinations of metals like Zn²⁺ and In³⁺ with imidazolate linkers will yield novel, hydrolytically stable ZIF frameworks with unique pore chemistries, leading to enhanced and tunable water sorption isotherms ideal for low-humidity harvesting.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput solvothermal synthesis to screen Zn/In ratios and linker combinations (e.g., imidazolate, 2-methylimidazolate). Advanced characterization (SCXRD, PXRD, TGA, N₂/H₂O sorption) will identify new phases. Water sorption performance will be evaluated at the single-crystal and bulk level using dynamic vapor sorption. We will utilize a ChatGPT-based AI assistant, trained on pre-2026 MOF databases and synthesis literature, to analyze trends, suggest promising synthetic conditions, and help correlate metal-linker combinations with observed topologies and properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a synthetic roadmap for mixed-metal ZIFs, delivering a new family of water-stable porous materials. It will directly contribute to the thermodynamics of water uptake in complex MOF systems and identify candidate materials with step-shaped or steep water isotherms for next-generation harvesting devices.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the singular example of ZIF-5, this project represents the first dedicated campaign to map the Zn/In-imidazolate phase space for water sorption, integrating AI-guided synthesis with precise single-crystal-level property analysis to deconvolute structure-function relationships.

proposals.title: In-situ Spectroscopic Mapping of Water and Alkali Metal Ion Uptake Dynamics in ZIF-8 and MOF-808
proposals.motivation: Understanding the molecular-level interactions of guest species, such as water and alkali metal ions, within the pores of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like ZIFs is critical for advancing applications in atmospheric water harvesting and selective ion capture. Pre-2026 studies, including those from the Yaghi group, have quantified uptake thermodynamics but lack direct, real-time spectroscopic evidence of the binding mechanisms and host-guest structural evolution during adsorption. Bridging this gap is essential for the rational design of next-generation sorbents with tailored kinetics and selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in-situ infrared (IR) and Raman spectroscopy will reveal distinct, reversible coordination modes and hydrogen-bonding networks for water and alkali metal ions within ZIF pores, directly correlating spectroscopic signatures with the stepwise filling and thermodynamic profiles observed in prior uptake studies.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-quality, phase-pure single crystals of ZIF-8 and MOF-808, leveraging established solvothermal protocols. Using a custom-designed, temperature- and humidity-controlled reaction cell coupled to FT-IR and confocal Raman microscopes, we will collect time-resolved spectra during controlled exposure to H2O vapor and aqueous alkali metal salt solutions. Spectral analysis will employ multivariate curve resolution (MCR) algorithms to deconvolute overlapping bands and principal component analysis (PCA) to identify key spectroscopic transitions corresponding to specific adsorption states and framework interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic spectroscopic map of guest-framework interactions during water and ion uptake in prototypical ZIFs, moving beyond bulk isotherms. It will establish a foundational library of in-situ spectroscopic fingerprints that can guide the AI-driven discovery and optimization of MOFs for separation and harvesting applications, as envisioned in pre-2026 AI4X roadmaps.
proposals.novelty: While in-situ spectroscopy is common in MOF catalysis, its targeted application to deconvolute the subtle, competitive interactions of water molecules and hydrated ions within the confined, hydrophobic pores of ZIFs during the adsorption process itself is a novel and non-obvious extension of the technique.

proposals.title: Covalent Post-Synthetic Modification of Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks for Enhanced Atmospheric Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks (ZIFs) are promising for atmospheric water harvesting due to their high stability, but their hydrophilicity and water uptake profiles are often fixed by synthesis. Pre-2026, covalent post-synthetic modification (PSM) has been a powerful tool to tailor Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) properties, yet its systematic application to ZIFs remains underexplored. Extending these techniques to ZIFs could unlock precise control over water sorption thermodynamics and kinetics, a critical need for next-generation harvesting materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that covalent PSM strategies, particularly amine- and azide-based 'click' chemistries, can be successfully applied to the imidazolate linkers of prototypical ZIFs (e.g., ZIF-8, ZIF-90) to install functional groups that modulate water affinity and uptake capacity without compromising framework integrity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize ZIF-8 and ZIF-90 with high crystallinity and subsequently subject them to established PSM reactions, such as the condensation of ZIF-90's aldehyde group with amines or the strain-promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition on pre-functionalized linkers. The modified ZIFs will be characterized by PXRD, NMR, and N2/water vapor sorption isotherms. Water harvesting performance will be evaluated at the single-crystal level using microgravimetric analysis to directly correlate introduced functionality with uptake capacity and cycle stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for the covalent functionalization of ZIFs, directly expanding the toolbox of reticular chemistry. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships linking specific chemical modifications to water sorption behavior, guiding the rational design of advanced sorbents. The project will yield new, tunable ZIF variants with optimized isotherms for efficient atmospheric water harvesting.
proposals.novelty: While PSM is mature for many MOFs, its application to ZIFs is non-trivial due to their different chemical stability and linker reactivity; systematically adapting these techniques for ZIFs is a novel synthetic challenge. The integration of single-crystal level water uptake measurements with precise chemical modification will provide unprecedented mechanistic insight into sorbent design.

proposals.title: Programmable Porosity via ZIF-Templated Synthesis: A Machine Learning-Guided Inverse Design Framework for Hierarchical Porous Materials
proposals.motivation: The controlled synthesis of porous materials with hierarchical, multi-scale porosity remains a significant challenge, limiting their efficiency in applications like catalysis, energy storage, and water harvesting. While ZIFs are renowned for their stability and designable pore chemistry, their use as sacrificial templates to imprint their precise porosity into new metal or carbon matrices is underexplored. This inverse design problem—starting with a desired pore architecture and identifying the optimal ZIF template and conversion conditions—is ideally suited for modern computational discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: That the distinct, tunable pore geometries and chemical environments of specific ZIF frameworks can be faithfully transferred to novel porous carbon or metal structures through controlled pyrolysis or chemical conversion, and that this template-to-product relationship can be modeled and inverted using machine learning to design materials with targeted porosity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a diverse library of ZIFs (e.g., ZIF-8, ZIF-67, ZIF-90) and subject them to systematic thermal and chemical treatments to create templated porous carbons and metals. High-resolution characterization (PXRD, gas sorption, TEM) will quantify the resulting pore metrics. A graph neural network (GNN) model will be trained on this dataset to learn the mapping from ZIF precursor features (metal node, linker, topology) and processing conditions to final material properties. This model will then be used in an inverse design loop, leveraging Bayesian optimization to recommend optimal ZIF templates and synthesis parameters for user-specified porosity targets.
