proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational 'porosity transfer' principle, demonstrating ZIFs as versatile templates beyond their native applications. It will produce a publicly accessible dataset and a predictive ML model that inverts the complex synthesis-property relationship, accelerating the discovery of hierarchically porous materials for gas separation and atmospheric water harvesting with pre-defined performance characteristics.
proposals.novelty: While ZIF-derived carbons are known, the systematic, inverse design approach—using ML to start from a desired pore architecture and work backwards to select a ZIF template and conversion protocol—is a novel paradigm shift. This moves beyond trial-and-error to a predictive, goal-oriented framework for templated material synthesis.

proposals.title: Amorphous Metal-Catecholate Frameworks: A High-Throughput AI-Guided Exploration for Catalytic Water Remediation
proposals.motivation: The exceptional catalytic properties of crystalline MOFs, such as MOF-808, are often attributed to their long-range order and well-defined active sites. However, their synthesis requires precise control, and their stability under harsh catalytic conditions can be limited. Exploring amorphous or partially ordered metal-catecholate polymers could reveal robust, synthetically accessible materials that bypass the need for crystalline perfection while maintaining or enhancing catalytic function, particularly for energy-intensive processes like water treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that amorphous metal-catecholate frameworks, with their inherent defect tolerance and tunable local coordination environments, can achieve catalytic performance for peroxide activation and pollutant degradation that rivals or exceeds their crystalline MOF counterparts, offering superior stability and scalability.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput synthesis robot to generate libraries of Fe(III)-, Cu(II)-, and Zr(IV)-catecholate polymers under varied conditions (pH, solvent, linker-to-metal ratio) to deliberately access amorphous and semicrystalline phases. Their local structure will be characterized by X-ray pair distribution function (PDF) analysis and XAS. Catalytic performance for peroxide activation will be benchmarked against crystalline MOF-808 using a model organic dye degradation assay. An AI model (a graph neural network trained on the Inorganic Crystal Structure Database and experimental data from our lab) will predict promising metal-linker combinations and local motifs for high activity, guiding subsequent synthesis iterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-property relationship for catalysis in amorphous coordination polymers, moving beyond the paradigm of crystalline order as a prerequisite for high performance. It will deliver a new class of robust, earth-abundant catalysts for water remediation and provide a validated AI-driven workflow for the discovery of non-crystalline functional materials.
proposals.novelty: While amorphous MOFs have been studied for gas sorption, their deliberate design and evaluation for liquid-phase catalytic applications, guided by AI predictions of local active sites rather than long-range order, represents a significant and non-obvious departure from Professor Yaghi's foundational work on crystalline frameworks.

proposals.title: Fe3-μ3-oxo Clusters as Primary Catalytic Sites in MOF-808 for Enhanced Water Harvesting and CO2 Reduction
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like MOF-808, which contain both Fe3-μ3-oxo trimers and installed molecular catalysts (e.g., Co-phthalocyanine), are typically designed with the molecular site as the primary catalytic center. However, the inherent redox activity and water-stability of the Fe3-oxo clusters, analogous to those in natural enzymes and minerals, remain underexploited for synergistic catalysis. This project aims to fundamentally shift the design paradigm to leverage these inorganic secondary building units (SBUs) as the main active sites, potentially unlocking more robust and efficient multifunctional materials for critical applications like atmospheric water harvesting and CO2 conversion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by synthetically tuning the coordination environment and electronic structure of the Fe3-μ3-oxo clusters in MOF-808, we can activate them as superior primary catalytic sites for the coupled capture and reduction of CO2 using water harvested from air, outperforming frameworks where catalysis is dominated by installed molecular complexes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOF-808 variants with modified Fe3 clusters via post-synthetic metalation and ligand exchange, while deliberately omitting or deactivating Co-phthalocyanine centers. Advanced characterization (in-situ XAS, Mössbauer spectroscopy, DRIFTS) will map the cluster's electronic states. Catalytic performance for CO2 reduction under controlled humidity will be evaluated via GC/MS. Concurrently, water adsorption isotherms will be measured. We will employ AI-driven analysis (specifically, graph neural networks trained on pre-2026 MOF databases) to correlate structural descriptors of the Fe3 cluster with adsorption and catalytic activity, guiding iterative synthesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for MOF catalysts by demonstrating the deliberate functionalization of inorganic SBUs as primary active sites. It will provide fundamental insights into the catalytic mechanisms of polynuclear metal-oxo clusters under humid conditions. The project is expected to yield a novel, integrated material platform for simultaneous atmospheric water harvesting and CO2 utilization, contributing to the global challenges of water scarcity and climate change.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in intentionally inverting the conventional hierarchy of active sites in heterogenized molecular-MOF systems, moving the typically 'structural' Fe3 SBU to a 'functional' catalytic role. This approach is distinct from prior work that focuses on modifying the installed molecular complex or uses the SBU merely as a supporting scaffold.

proposals.title: Electrocatalytic Performance of Co-MOF-1992 for Oxygen Reduction and Hydrogen Evolution Reactions
proposals.motivation: The development of efficient, non-precious metal electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is critical for advancing clean energy technologies like fuel cells and water electrolyzers. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with accessible, redox-active metal sites offer a promising platform for such catalysts, but systematic evaluation of their intrinsic activity remains a challenge. MOF-1992, a cobalt-based framework with well-defined, coordinatively unsaturated Co(II) sites, has shown promise for water harvesting and ion uptake, but its electrocatalytic potential is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the accessible, unsaturated cobalt sites in MOF-1992 will serve as efficient, stable active centers for electrocatalytic ORR and HER, exhibiting competitive activity and selectivity compared to state-of-the-art non-precious metal catalysts.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phase-pure, high-quality single crystals of MOF-1992 and fabricate them into thin-film electrodes. Electrocatalytic performance for ORR and HER will be evaluated in aqueous electrolytes using rotating disk electrode voltammetry to measure activity, Tafel slopes, and electron transfer numbers. Post-catalysis characterization via X-ray absorption spectroscopy and electron microscopy will assess structural integrity and oxidation state of the Co sites. AI-assisted analysis of electrochemical data, using established machine learning models (e.g., gradient boosting regressors) as outlined in pre-2026 AI4X roadmaps, will correlate structural descriptors with catalytic metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the fundamental electrocatalytic profile of MOF-1992, providing quantitative structure-activity relationships for cobalt-based MOFs in energy conversion. It will deliver a new, well-characterized candidate material for ORR and HER catalysts and expand the application scope of hydrostable MOFs beyond adsorption to include electrocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-1992's gas and water adsorption properties are known, its application as a defined, single-crystal electrode for benchmarking intrinsic electrocatalytic activity at molecular metal sites is unexplored and non-obvious, moving beyond composite or pyrolyzed MOF-derived catalysts.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Pore Engineering in MOF-1992 via Zeolite-Inspired Strategies for Enhanced Water and Ion Transport
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like MOF-1992 (HKUST-1) show immense promise for water harvesting and ion uptake, but their performance in dynamic adsorption processes is often limited by slow mass transport within purely microporous networks. This bottleneck hinders their practical application in atmospheric water harvesting and rapid ion exchange. Zeolite science has long addressed similar challenges by creating hierarchical pore structures to improve diffusion kinetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing hierarchical porosity—combining micropores with mesopores—into MOF-1992 will significantly enhance the mass transport of water vapor and alkali metal ions without compromising its intrinsic high adsorption capacity and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will apply zeolite-inspired pore engineering strategies to MOF-1992. First, we will synthesize hierarchical variants using a modulated synthesis approach with competing coordination modulators (e.g., monocarboxylic acids) to create intrinsic mesoporosity. Second, we will employ post-synthetic linker thermolysis, a method analogous to zeolite dealumination, to selectively remove a fraction of the organic linkers, generating interconnected mesopores. The materials will be characterized via N2 physisorption, electron microscopy, and X-ray diffraction. Their performance will be evaluated using gravimetric water adsorption/desorption cycling and batch uptake experiments for alkali metal ions (e.g., Li+, Na+), with kinetics modeled using established diffusion equations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct materials design link between zeolite pore engineering and MOF functionality, providing a generalizable synthetic blueprint for overcoming mass transport limitations in dense MOF networks. It will yield a new class of hierarchical MOF-1992 materials with quantified enhancements in water harvesting rates and ion uptake kinetics, advancing their potential for real-world applications.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical MOFs exist, the systematic translation of specific zeolite pore-creation strategies (like dealumination analogs) to a well-studied, commercially relevant MOF like MOF-1992 for dual water and ion transport applications is novel. This approach leverages deep zeolite knowledge to solve a persistent MOF engineering problem.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Linker Functionalization in Zirconium MOFs for Enhanced Hydrogen and Methane Storage
proposals.motivation: The widespread adoption of clean energy vectors like hydrogen and natural gas is limited by safe, high-density storage technologies. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer a promising solution, but their gas uptake capacities and binding strengths require precise optimization. Pre-2026 work has shown that linker functionalization can tune pore chemistry, yet a systematic, predictive approach to designing optimal functional groups is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted functionalization of conjugated organic linkers in robust zirconium MOFs (e.g., UiO-66, MOF-808 analogs) with specific electron-donating/withdrawing groups will create optimal binding sites, thereby significantly enhancing the volumetric and gravimetric uptake of H2 and CH4 at ambient and cryogenic temperatures.
proposals.method: We will employ a closed-loop, AI-driven workflow. First, a generative deep learning model (a variational autoencoder conditioned on gas uptake properties) will propose novel functionalized linker structures. These will be synthesized and incorporated into Zr-MOFs, leveraging Prof. Yaghi's established reticular chemistry techniques. High-pressure gas sorption isotherms and in-situ spectroscopic characterization will provide experimental validation. The resulting data will be used to iteratively refine a graph neural network model, creating a predictive map between functional group, framework topology, and gas storage performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of high-performance, stable MOFs with record gas storage capacities under practical conditions. More importantly, it will establish a validated AI/experimental framework for the rational design of functionalized porous materials, moving beyond trial-and-error. The open-sourced model and dataset will accelerate discovery for related challenges like carbon capture.
proposals.novelty: While AI has been used to screen known MOF databases, this proposal uniquely integrates generative AI for *de novo* linker design with high-throughput synthesis and validation, focusing on the underexplored chemical space of conjugated linker functionalization in ultra-stable Zr-MOF platforms.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Discovery of MOFs for the Selective Capture and Storage of Nitrous Oxide and Acetylene
proposals.motivation: Beyond H2, CO2, and CH4, other industrially relevant gases present critical challenges. Nitrous oxide (N2O) is a potent greenhouse gas and ozone-depleting substance, while acetylene (C2H2) is a crucial chemical feedstock whose purification from CO2 is energy-intensive. Pre-2026, the systematic exploration of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for these targets is limited, representing a significant materials gap.
proposals.hypothesis: That a high-throughput computational screening, guided by artificial intelligence, will identify MOF-808 variants and other zirconium-based MOFs with exceptional uptake and selectivity for N2O and C2H2, driven by specific metal-node and linker modifications that optimize host-guest interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale AI and simulation approach. First, a generative AI model (a fine-tuned GPT architecture) will propose synthetically accessible linker modifications for MOF-808 and related Zr-MOFs. Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations will then calculate adsorption isotherms and selectivity for N2O/N2, N2O/O2, and C2H2/CO2 mixtures. These results will train a graph neural network (GNN) to predict performance from structure, creating a rapid screening loop. The most promising candidates will be synthesized and their adsorption properties validated using volumetric and gravimetric analyzers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationships for N2O and C2H2 capture in tunable Zr-MOFs, delivering new high-performance sorbents. It will also demonstrate a closed-loop AI-driven workflow for MOF discovery beyond the most common gas targets, accelerating materials development for under-addressed separations.
proposals.novelty: The focus on N2O and C2H2 as primary targets for MOF design is novel. Furthermore, integrating generative AI for linker design specifically within the stable MOF-808 platform for these gases moves beyond post-screening analysis to predictive, guided synthesis.

proposals.title: A Reticular Design Strategy for Non-Interpenetrated Metal-Organic Frameworks with Predictable Topology and Enhanced Porosity
proposals.motivation: Interpenetration in Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) is a persistent challenge that significantly reduces accessible pore volume and surface area, undermining their performance in critical applications like gas storage, water harvesting, and catalysis. While serendipitous synthesis has yielded some non-interpenetrated structures, a rational, predictive design strategy is lacking. This project aims to establish a foundational principle in reticular chemistry to systematically avoid interpenetration, thereby unlocking the full theoretical porosity of designed networks.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing molecular building blocks (MBBs) with rigid, bulky, and strategically angled geometries, we can enforce the formation of MOF topologies with inherent steric and geometric constraints that thermodynamically and kinetically disfavor the nucleation and growth of a second, interpenetrating network.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental reticular chemistry approach. First, we will use in-house and published topological databases (e.g., the Reticular Chemistry Structure Resource) to identify known non-interpenetrated nets. Using geometric analysis and molecular mechanics simulations (e.g., with Materials Studio or analogous pre-2026 software), we will parameterize design rules linking MBB shape (e.g., trigonal planar, tetrahedral, octahedral) and size to the resulting cavity dimensions and window apertures. These rules will guide the synthesis of new, custom-designed organic linkers with extended, rigid, and sterically hindered backbones (e.g., based on triptycene, spirobifluorene, or other polycyclic units) to pair with metal clusters like Zr6O8 for MOF-808 analogs. Synthesis will be monitored at the single-crystal level to track nucleation events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a set of design principles for predicting and synthesizing non-interpenetrated MOFs, moving the field beyond trial-and-error. It will yield new, high-porosity MOF materials with validated, predictable topologies. The resulting frameworks will serve as superior platforms for studying fundamental thermodynamics of guest uptake, as referenced in recent work on water and alkali metal ions, due to their unhindered pore spaces.
proposals.novelty: While reticular chemistry is established for predicting topology, its deliberate application to *prevent* interpenetration through proactive MBB design is a novel inversion of the problem. This approach integrates steric design directly into the reticular synthesis blueprint, rather than relying on post-synthetic modifications or serendipitous crystallization conditions.

proposals.title: Kinetically Trapped, Non-Interpenetrated MOF-808 via Modulator-Guided Nucleation for Enhanced Water and Ion Uptake
proposals.motivation: The formation of interpenetrated frameworks in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like MOF-808 is a persistent challenge, as it reduces pore volume and accessibility, directly limiting performance in critical applications such as atmospheric water harvesting and ion uptake from aqueous solutions. Pre-2026 work has demonstrated the high water uptake of MOF-808 but also highlighted thermodynamic favorability for interpenetration, which compromises its theoretical capacity. Controlling crystallization to avoid interpenetration remains a fundamental materials design problem with significant implications for the performance of porous materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying crystal engineering principles to carefully control the initial nucleation event using tailored modulators and synthetic conditions, we can kinetically trap MOF-808 in its non-interpenetrated form, thereby maximizing its usable porosity.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, modulator-screening approach using formate, acetate, and benzoate modulators of varying concentration and acidity to influence nucleation kinetics during the solvothermal synthesis of MOF-808. Reaction progress will be monitored in situ using techniques like powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) to correlate modulator identity with nucleation rate and phase purity. The resulting materials will be characterized for permanent porosity (N2, 77K), water adsorption isotherms, and alkali metal ion uptake capacity, with structural confirmation via single-crystal and Rietveld-refined PXRD analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct synthetic strategy to produce phase-pure, non-interpenetrated MOF-808, providing a clear structure-property relationship between controlled nucleation, absence of interpenetration, and enhanced uptake performance. It will deliver a generalizable crystal engineering blueprint for kinetic trapping of desired metastable MOF phases, moving beyond thermodynamic product prediction.
proposals.novelty: While modulators are known to control crystal size and morphology, their targeted use to dictate framework topology (interpenetrated vs. non-interpenetrated) by governing the very first nucleation events is a novel and underexplored application of crystal engineering principles in MOF synthesis.

proposals.title: Active-Linker MOFs: Engineering Organic Struts as Primary Adsorption Sites for Enhanced Atmospheric Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: In MOFs, the organic linkers have traditionally served as passive structural elements, with adsorption driven by open metal sites or pore confinement. This limits the design space for sorbents, particularly for polar molecules like water where tunable organic chemistry could offer superior control over uptake thermodynamics and kinetics. A paradigm shift to treat the linker as the primary, active adsorption site could unlock new materials with unprecedented performance for critical applications like atmospheric water harvesting.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically functionalizing the organic linkers in robust, hydrolytically stable MOFs (e.g., Zr-based) with precisely arranged, high-density polar and hydrogen-bonding groups, we can invert the primary adsorption role from the metal clusters to the organic matrix, creating materials where water uptake is dominated by linker-water interactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOF-808 analogues where the benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylate (BTC) linker is systematically replaced with custom-synthesized linkers bearing dense arrays of amide, hydroxyl, or amine functionalities. Water adsorption isotherms and enthalpies will be measured at the single-crystal level using microcalorimetry and gravimetry. AI-assisted analysis (using graph neural networks trained on pre-2026 MOF databases) will correlate linker chemical features with adsorption metrics to guide iterative synthesis, focusing on achieving steep uptake at low relative humidity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for MOFs, moving beyond the metal-centric view of adsorption. It will deliver a novel class of water-harvesting materials whose performance is dictated by programmable organic chemistry, potentially achieving higher capacity at lower humidity than state-of-the-art metal-site-based sorbents. The AI-guided structure-property map will serve as a blueprint for 'active-linker' MOF design for other guest molecules.
proposals.novelty: While linker functionalization is common, the explicit design goal of making the organic strut the *primary* and *thermodynamically dominant* adsorption site, effectively inverting the established role of MOF components, is a non-obvious and unexplored strategy. This approach decouples stability (provided by the robust metal cluster) from tunable adsorption (provided by the organic linker).

proposals.title: Systematic Expansion of the Isoreticular MOF Series via Secondary Building Unit Substitution: From Zn4O to Zr6, Fe3, and Al3 Clusters
proposals.motivation: The isoreticular principle, pioneered using the Zn4O(CO2)6 cluster, revolutionized porous materials design by enabling predictable pore size and functionality variation. However, its application has been largely confined to this single, relatively labile cluster type, limiting the chemical stability and catalytic potential of the resulting frameworks. Extending this powerful design strategy to more robust and catalytically active metal-oxygen clusters (e.g., Zr6, Fe3-oxo) would unlock new families of MOFs with enhanced stability for applications like water harvesting and ion uptake, while introducing diverse, tunable metal sites for catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the isoreticular design principle can be successfully generalized to other high-valence, carboxylate-linked metal-oxygen clusters, enabling the synthesis of new, topologically identical MOF series with vastly improved chemical stability and distinct functional properties dictated by the intrinsic nature of the secondary building unit (SBU).
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted synthesis approach, selecting robust SBUs like Zr6O4(OH)4(CO2)12 (from MOF-808), Fe3O(CO2)6, and Al3O(CO2)6. Using pre-2026 AI tools, specifically fine-tuned large language models (e.g., ChatGPT-4) and graph neural networks trained on the Cambridge Structural Database, we will predict linker geometries and synthesis conditions compatible with each target SBU to maintain the desired net topology. High-throughput, single-crystal-level synthesis and characterization (PXRD, gas/vapor sorption) will validate the predicted structures and assess properties like water stability and uptake.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalized framework for isoreticular chemistry beyond Zn4O, creating multiple new families of ultra-stable, functionally diverse MOFs. It will provide a systematic understanding of how SBU identity dictates framework stability and function, directly impacting fields requiring robust porous materials, such as atmospheric water harvesting and selective ion capture from aqueous solutions.
proposals.novelty: While isoreticular expansion within a given SBU is well-established, the deliberate, systematic substitution of the foundational SBU itself to create new, topologically consistent series is a non-obvious and unexplored frontier that merges classic MOF design with the stability of inorganic clusters.

proposals.title: Molecular-Level Probing of Water-Framework Interactions in Hydrolytically Stable MOFs via In-Situ Spectroscopy and AI-Enhanced Analysis
proposals.motivation: The molecular-level mechanism of water adsorption and transport in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is critical for advancing atmospheric water harvesting and selective separations. While macroscopic uptake isotherms are well-studied, the specific binding sites, hydrogen-bonding networks, and framework dynamics during hydration remain poorly understood, limiting the rational design of next-generation sorbents. Pre-2026, the integration of advanced in-situ characterization with computational models presents a timely opportunity to bridge this knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in-situ spectroscopic techniques will reveal distinct, sequential binding events of water molecules at the node and linker sites of hydrolytically stable MOFs (e.g., MOF-303, MOF-808), and that these interactions can be correlated with thermodynamic profiles to establish a predictive structure-function model.
proposals.method: We will employ in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and Raman spectroscopy under controlled relative humidity to track the evolution of O-H and framework vibrational modes in real time. Synchrotron-based in-situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) will probe local metal-node geometry changes. These spectroscopic datasets will be analyzed using a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, trained to deconvolute spectral features and assign them to specific molecular interactions, thereby linking spectral signatures to adsorption energetics derived from prior thermodynamic studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, molecular-scale map of water adsorption pathways in leading hydrolytically stable MOFs. It will establish a foundational spectroscopic database and an AI-assisted analytical framework for interpreting complex in-situ data. The resulting insights will directly inform the synthesis of new materials with optimized water uptake kinetics and capacity for harvesting applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of multiple in-situ surface science probes specifically targeted at water-MOF interactions, coupled with an AI-driven analysis pipeline to decode the complex, time-resolved spectroscopic data into a coherent mechanistic model, moving beyond correlative studies to causal understanding.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Metal-Organic Framework Linkers for Programmable Pore Geometries via Generative Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: The rational design of MOFs for applications like water harvesting and gas storage is currently limited by a forward-design paradigm, where a linker is synthesized and the resulting pore geometry is characterized. This trial-and-error approach is inefficient for achieving precise, application-specific pore environments. An inverse design method, which starts from a target pore geometry and identifies the necessary organic linker, would dramatically accelerate the discovery of MOFs with optimized properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative deep learning model, trained on a large database of known MOF structures, can learn the complex mapping between pore geometry descriptors and the chemical structure of its organic linkers, enabling the computational design of novel linkers for user-defined pore targets.
proposals.method: We will curate a large dataset of MOF structures from pre-2026 databases (e.g., CSD, hypothetical MOFs). Pore geometry will be characterized using topological descriptors (pore size distribution, surface area, void fraction) and geometric hashing of pore shape. A conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) or a graph-based generative adversarial network (GAN) will be trained to generate SMILES strings or molecular graphs of linkers conditioned on these pore descriptors. The generated linkers will be validated through classical molecular mechanics simulations (e.g., using RASPA) to assess the stability and fidelity of the resulting MOF structure to the target pore.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational computational pipeline for the inverse design of MOF building blocks, moving the field beyond serendipitous discovery. It will provide a new AI-driven tool for rapidly proposing candidate MOFs with pores tailored for specific targets, such as optimal water uptake isotherms or selective ion binding sites, directly addressing themes in Yaghi's recent work on water harvesting and ion uptake.
proposals.novelty: While AI has been used to predict MOF properties from structure, the inverse problem—generating chemically realistic linker structures from a geometric pore target—remains largely unexplored. This approach inverts the conventional design logic for porous materials.

proposals.title: Inverted Metal-Organic Frameworks (iMOFs): Organic Nodes as Coordination Hubs for Metal-Oxide Struts
proposals.motivation: The canonical MOF architecture, with inorganic metal clusters as nodes and organic linkers as struts, has dominated reticular chemistry. However, this paradigm inherently limits the chemical and electronic diversity of the framework nodes. Inverting this connectivity to use robust, multi-dentate organic molecules as nodes and metal-oxygen clusters (e.g., secondary building units like Zr6O8) as struts could unlock frameworks with unprecedented node functionality, potentially leading to novel catalytic, electronic, or sorption properties not accessible in conventional MOFs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically designed, high-connectivity organic molecules with multiple, spatially separated chelating sites can act as stable nodes to coordinate pre-formed metal-oxygen clusters, forming extended, crystalline frameworks with inverted topology and distinct chemical environments at the pore walls.
proposals.method: The project will employ a combined computational and experimental workflow. First, we will use generative AI models (specifically, fine-tuned GPT architectures and graph neural networks trained on the Cambridge Structural Database) to design and screen candidate organic node molecules with 4- to 8-connectivity, prioritizing rigid, redox-active, or photoactive cores like porphyrins or cyanurates. Predicted structures will be assessed for synthetic viability and stability via DFT calculations. Experimentally, we will synthesize target nodes and react them with pre-formed, carboxylate-functionalized metal-oxygen clusters (e.g., Zr6O4(OH)4(acrylate)12) under solvothermal conditions, using high-throughput robotic synthesis to explore parameter space. Characterization will involve single-crystal X-ray diffraction, gas and water vapor sorption, and solid-state NMR.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for reticular materials, expanding the conceptual toolbox of MOF chemistry. It will deliver the first proven examples of crystalline iMOFs, providing fundamental insights into their stability and bonding. The resulting materials will be screened for applications in water harvesting and metal-ion uptake, testing if the inverted architecture offers advantages in binding kinetics or selectivity.
proposals.novelty: While postsynthetic modification of MOF nodes is common, the core construction logic of using organic molecules as the primary, high-connectivity structural nodes for metal-based struts is a fundamental inversion of the established MOF paradigm, representing a new branch of reticular chemistry.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Synthesis of Hydrolytically and Thermally Stable MOFs for Atmospheric Water Harvesting
proposals.motivation: The practical deployment of MOFs in applications like atmospheric water harvesting and gas storage is critically limited by their instability under humid and thermal cycling conditions. While MOF-808 shows promising water uptake, its long-term structural integrity in real-world, fluctuating environments remains a key challenge. Enhancing stability without compromising capacity is essential for transitioning these materials from laboratory demonstrations to field-deployable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted, AI-predicted modifications to the metal node and organic linker chemistry of zirconium-based MOFs (like MOF-808) will yield new frameworks with significantly enhanced hydrolytic and thermal stability while maintaining high water adsorption capacity.
proposals.method: We will employ a closed-loop, AI-guided synthesis platform. A generative deep learning model (a variational autoencoder conditioned on stability descriptors) will propose novel MOF candidates with modified linkers (e.g., incorporating hydrophobic functional groups) and node alternatives (e.g., mixed-metal Zr/Ti clusters). High-throughput, robotic synthesis (leveraging 'synthesis at the single-crystal level' precision) will create these candidates. Their stability will be rigorously tested using accelerated aging protocols in humid environments and thermogravimetric analysis, with the resulting data fed back to refine the AI model iteratively.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of ultra-stable, high-performance MOFs specifically designed for harsh-condition applications. It will establish an AI-driven design paradigm for material stability, moving beyond porosity and capacity. The resulting materials and protocols will provide a critical pathway for practical MOF-based devices in water harvesting and gas separation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of generative AI with targeted experimental validation to solve the stability problem, a persistent bottleneck. Instead of post-synthetic modifications or trial-and-error, we use AI to fundamentally redesign the MOF's chemical building blocks for inherent robustness, guided by real thermodynamic and degradation data.

proposals.title: AI-Guided Design of Isoreticular MOFs for Enhanced Ambient-Temperature Hydrogen Storage
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-capacity hydrogen storage at near-ambient temperatures remains a critical bottleneck for the widespread adoption of hydrogen fuel cell technologies. While MOFs have shown exceptional promise due to their high surface areas and tunability, most high-performing materials require cryogenic temperatures (77 K) for significant uptake. The U.S. Department of Energy's 2025 system targets for onboard storage demand new materials that operate under more practical conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically integrating unsaturated metal sites (UMCs) and optimizing pore chemistry within a single, highly tunable isoreticular platform (e.g., MOF-808 or UiO-66), we can design MOFs that achieve significantly enhanced hydrogen binding enthalpies (15-25 kJ/mol) through a combination of Kubas-type interactions and optimal pore confinement, leading to higher volumetric and gravimetric capacities at 298 K and moderate pressures (50-100 bar).
proposals.method: We will employ a closed-loop, AI-driven design strategy. First, a generative deep learning model (a variational autoencoder conditioned on pore geometry and chemical descriptors) will propose novel, synthetically plausible linkers and metal-cluster functionalizations for the Zr6-based isoreticular platform. These candidates will be screened via high-throughput Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations for hydrogen adsorption at 298 K. Promising candidates will be synthesized, and their hydrogen uptake will be measured using high-pressure volumetric analyzers. The experimental data will be fed back into the AI model to refine future design cycles, accelerating the discovery process.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of isoreticular MOFs with experimentally validated, superior ambient-temperature hydrogen storage performance. It will establish a robust AI/experimental framework for the targeted design of porous materials for energy storage, moving beyond brute-force screening. The resulting structure-property relationships will provide fundamental insights into the synergistic roles of UMCs and pore environment in enhancing H2 binding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the focused, AI-guided exploration of a single, highly stable isoreticular series to systematically engineer multiple, cooperative binding sites, rather than screening vast chemical spaces of disparate structures. This approach directly integrates generative AI for de novo linker design with precise experimental validation at the single-crystal level, as demonstrated in the PI's recent work on water harvesting.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Virtual Screening of Metal-Organic Frameworks for Atmospheric Water Harvesting Using Pharmaceutical Discovery Algorithms
proposals.motivation: The development of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for atmospheric water harvesting (AWH) is critical for addressing global water scarcity, but the vast chemical space of possible MOF structures makes experimental exploration slow and resource-intensive. Pre-2026, computational screening has been used, but often lacks the sophisticated, multi-parameter optimization and rapid iteration cycles standard in modern pharmaceutical discovery for drug candidates. Adapting these proven methodologies to MOF discovery could dramatically accelerate the identification of next-generation water-harvesting materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-throughput virtual screening (HTVS) pipelines and quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) models, adapted from pharmaceutical lead optimization, can efficiently identify MOF structures with superior water uptake capacity and optimal adsorption isotherm shapes for energy-efficient AWH under realistic desert conditions.
proposals.method: We will adapt a modular HTVS pipeline analogous to those used for small-molecule drug discovery. A curated library of hypothetical and experimentally realized MOFs will serve as the 'compound' library. Key performance descriptors (e.g., water uptake at specific relative humidities, pore volume, isosteric heat of adsorption) will be calculated using grand canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations as the primary 'assay'. Machine learning models, including graph neural networks (GNNs) and random forests, will be trained on initial simulation data to establish a QSAR model predicting performance from structural fingerprints. This model will then guide an iterative screening and 'lead' MOF optimization cycle, focusing on zirconium and aluminum-based clusters for stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated computational pipeline for the rapid discovery of AWH-MOFs, identifying at least three promising, synthetically accessible lead structures with predicted performance exceeding state-of-the-art materials like MOF-303. It will establish a novel cross-disciplinary methodology, applying pharmaceutical informatics to porous material science, and provide a public dataset linking MOF structures to detailed water adsorption properties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in high-throughput screening itself, but in the direct adaptation of the specific iterative workflows, validation protocols, and QSAR modeling paradigms from pharmaceutical discovery—a field with decades of refinement in multi-property optimization—to the challenge of porous material design for water harvesting.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Synthesis and Water Harvesting Performance of ZIFs with Non-Traditional Tetrahedral Metal Nodes
proposals.motivation: Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks (ZIFs) based on Zn and Co have shown remarkable stability and water adsorption properties, crucial for atmospheric water harvesting. However, the chemical space of ZIFs with other tetrahedrally coordinating metals (e.g., Mn, Fe, Cu, Cd) remains largely unexplored, potentially hiding materials with superior uptake capacities, optimal hydrophilicity, or lower cost. A systematic exploration is needed to map the thermodynamic stability and water adsorption profiles of these novel ZIFs to identify next-generation water harvesting materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ZIFs constructed from non-traditional tetrahedral metal nodes (Mn(II), Fe(II), Cu(I), Cd(II)) will exhibit distinct water adsorption thermodynamics and capacities compared to Zn/Co-ZIFs, due to variations in metal-linker bond strength, polarizability, and framework flexibility, leading to the discovery of new materials with enhanced water harvesting performance.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, robotic synthesis platform to synthesize ZIFs with imidazolate and functionalized imidazolate linkers using Mn, Fe, Cu, and Cd salts under solvothermal conditions. The phase purity and stability of resulting crystals will be characterized via PXRD and TGA. Water adsorption isotherms will be measured at the single-crystal level using a microbalance-equipped environmental chamber, as demonstrated in recent work. The data will be integrated into a growing materials database and analyzed using machine learning models (e.g., gradient-boosted decision trees) to correlate metal node properties (ionic radius, electronegativity) with key adsorption metrics (uptake at low RH, step pressure, enthalpy).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive library of water adsorption data for non-Zn/Co ZIFs, directly expanding the materials toolkit for atmospheric water harvesting. It will yield fundamental structure-property relationships linking metal identity to adsorption thermodynamics, guiding the rational design of future sorbents. The generated dataset will be a valuable resource for the AI-driven prediction of porous material properties.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput synthesis of MOFs is established, its focused application to systematically explore the largely untapped metal node space in ZIFs for water adsorption is novel. Combining this synthesis with single-crystal-level adsorption analysis and AI-assisted pattern recognition to decode metal-node influence represents a significant methodological advance beyond conventional Zn/Co-ZIF studies.

proposals.title: Robust Monolithic ZIF-8 and ZIF-67 Structures via Solvent-Free Binder Shaping for High-Performance Water and Ion Uptake Columns
proposals.motivation: Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks (ZIFs) like ZIF-8 and ZIF-67 show exceptional promise for atmospheric water harvesting and ion uptake from aqueous solutions, as evidenced by recent single-crystal-level studies. However, their practical deployment in adsorption columns is hindered by the poor processability of fine powders, which lead to high pressure drops and mechanical instability. Developing scalable shaping methods to form robust, hierarchically porous monoliths while preserving intrinsic porosity is a critical materials engineering challenge for real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a solvent-free, high-pressure compaction process using minimal amounts of judiciously selected polymeric binders (e.g., polyvinylpyrrolidone, polyvinyl alcohol) can produce mechanically robust ZIF monoliths with preserved microporosity and introduced meso/macropores, enabling efficient fluid transport and high adsorption capacity in column operations.
proposals.method: We will synthesize ZIF-8 and ZIF-67 powders and systematically screen polymeric binders for compatibility. Using a uniaxial press, we will optimize compaction pressure, binder type, and concentration (0.5-5 wt%) to form cylindrical monoliths. The monolithic structures will be characterized by XRD, SEM, and N2 porosimetry to assess crystallinity, morphology, and hierarchical porosity. Mechanical strength will be tested via crush strength measurements. Performance will be evaluated in custom adsorption columns for water vapor uptake (using microgravimetric analysis) and alkali metal ion (e.g., Li+, Na+) uptake from aqueous solutions, comparing kinetics and capacity to powder analogues.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable, practical shaping protocol for ZIF materials, directly translating fundamental adsorption properties into engineered forms suitable for device integration. It will provide a foundational materials processing framework for the construction of efficient adsorption columns for atmospheric water harvesting and water purification, bridging the gap between laboratory powder samples and industrial-scale applications.
proposals.novelty: While shaping of MOFs is known, a dedicated, systematic study focusing on the solvent-free monolithic shaping of ZIFs—a subclass with high chemical stability—for dual application in water vapor and ion uptake columns is novel. The approach prioritizes minimal binder use and hierarchical pore structure creation, which is non-trivial for maintaining the high surface area critical for ZIF performance.

ideas.title: Kinetically Controlled Irreversible Synthesis of Crystalline PGFs for Programmable Template Removal
ideas.description: This research direction combines the need for crystalline porous graphene frameworks (PGFs) with the use of irreversible coupling reactions, achieved through precise kinetic control of monomer addition and reaction conditions to guide crystallization. The resulting crystalline PGFs possess permanent, well-defined pores formed by irreversible covalent bonds, granting them exceptional chemical and thermal stability. These robust frameworks can then be used as sacrificial or durable templates for synthesizing nanomaterials within their pores. Crucially, because the template itself is irreversibly formed, it can be removed under harsh conditions (e.g., strong acids/bases, high temperature) or left in place as a composite component, allowing the templated synthesis of nanomaterials that would themselves disrupt reversible dynamic covalent networks.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided Tuning of Heterocyclic Dynamic Covalent Equilibria for Adaptive Coordination Frameworks
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a simple extension by establishing a design rule for dynamic heterocycles. It employs DFT calculations to predict the exchange kinetics and thermodynamics of C=N, B–N, or C–O bonds in diverse aromatic cores, identifying candidates with tunable dynamicity. The top computational hits are then synthesized as ditopic ligands with electronically diverse substituents, enabling systematic experimental correlation between substituent Hammett parameters and exchange rates. This validated framework allows for the rational design of new adaptive porous materials and self-healing polymers with precisely engineered environmental responsiveness.

ideas.title: Investigate MOF Separation via Selective Gating Inspired by Ion Channel Proteins
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from structural biology to the context of MOF-based separations. In biophysics, ion channel proteins achieve exquisite selectivity (e.g., for K+ over Na+) through a precisely arranged 'selectivity filter' that uses coordinated chemical interactions for gating and discrimination. The adapted idea is to move beyond simple size-exclusion in the new MOF by deliberately functionalizing its pores to mimic this biological principle. The research would involve designing and synthesizing MOF variants with specific, strategically placed chemical groups along the pore walls. These groups would act as a synthetic selectivity filter, creating a preferential diffusion pathway for one target gas or liquid molecule over another based on transient binding interactions, molecular shape recognition, or differential stabilization of diffusion transition states, thereby solving separation challenges where molecular sizes are very similar.

ideas.title: Synthesize PGF monolayers via a surfactant-templated interfacial polymerization
ideas.description: Scale down synthesis to produce exfoliated, monolayer crystalline porous graphitic frameworks by drawing an analogy from the field of Colloidal and Polymer Chemistry for 2D Nanosheet Synthesis. The analogous solution involves using surfactant-templated interfacial polymerization to synthesize monolayer COFs/MOFs, where the interface confines growth to two dimensions. The adapted idea applies this interfacial confinement strategy to PGF synthesis: a surfactant monolayer at an air-water or liquid-liquid interface is used to pre-organize polycyclic aromatic precursors. Catalytic cyclodehydrogenation or coupling reactions are then initiated at this 2D plane, with the surfactant template directing the formation of a continuous, covalent, porous graphitic network while suppressing multilayer growth. The resulting free-floating monolayer PGF sheet can be subsequently transferred to various substrates for characterization and application.

ideas.title: Applying Dynamic Covalent Exchange to Heteroaromatic C–X Bonds via a Catalytic, Metal-Free Transetherification Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the dynamic C=N bond strategy by drawing an analogy from polymer chemistry, specifically the design of vitrimers. In that field, catalyst-activated transetherification of C–O bonds enables dynamic, yet stable, covalent networks. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing the search for organocatalytic conditions (e.g., using Lewis acidic boron catalysts or Brønsted acids) that can reversibly cleave and reform other heteroatom linkages, such as C–O in furan-based systems or C–S in thiophene-based systems. This approach would generalize the dynamic covalent chemistry principle to a wider array of aromatic heterocycles, moving beyond nitrogen to oxygen and sulfur, for the creation of novel adaptive organic frameworks and materials.

ideas.title: Closed-loop computational prediction and experimental synthesis of targeted MOF phases
ideas.description: This project establishes a directed, iterative pipeline for MOF discovery by tightly coupling computational prediction with experimental synthesis. A machine learning model, trained on existing MOF databases and quantum chemical calculations, predicts the stability and functional properties of hypothetical MOF structures derived from a universal design strategy. These predictions guide high-throughput robotic synthesis towards the most promising candidates. The experimentally characterized outcomes—both successes and failures—are then fed back into the computational model to continuously refine its accuracy and focus the search for new phases with pre-specified performance metrics.

ideas.title: Iterative Computational-Experimental Refinement of PGF Structures via NMR-Constrained CSP
ideas.description: This research direction establishes an iterative cycle between computation and experiment specifically for porous glass films (PGFs). Predicted polymorphs from DFT/MD-based CSP are used to simulate their expected solid-state NMR spectra (chemical shifts, quadrupolar parameters, relaxation times). These simulations are directly compared to experimental NMR data, with discrepancies used to refine the computational force fields and search algorithms. The refined CSP then generates new, more accurate candidate structures, which are again tested against NMR, converging on a high-fidelity model that captures both long-range order and crucial local disorder, bonding, and ion dynamics critical for PGF function.

ideas.title: Kinetic Landscape Mapping and Active Steering for 2D Framework Crystallization
ideas.description: This project moves beyond static thermodynamic guidelines to address the kinetic bottlenecks that prevent 2D porous framework crystallization. It develops a closed-loop experimental platform that uses in-situ characterization to identify and map common kinetic failure modes, such as amorphous aggregation or polycrystalline domain formation, in real time. An active control algorithm then modulates synthesis parameters to navigate the energy landscape, selectively suppressing detrimental pathways and promoting the growth of single-crystalline domains. The output is a validated, dynamic protocol for achieving long-range order that explicitly incorporates kinetic control into the design rules for reversible 2D assembly.

ideas.title: Crystallization via Irreversible Assembly with Templated Kinetics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that reversible covalent bonds are necessary for crystallization by adapting a solution from biomineralization. In nature, organisms create perfect crystalline structures like nacre through irreversible precipitation, guided by organic templates that control local chemistry and spatial confinement. The adapted research idea is to use a pre-organized, supramolecular template (e.g., a crystalline or liquid-crystalline scaffold) to define the target lattice for a PGF. Irreversible covalent polymerization of the PGF monomers is then initiated with precise kinetic control (via light, catalyst, or temperature) within this template. The reaction propagates in register with the template's periodicity, effectively 'casting' the crystalline order into the irreversible covalent polymer network, thereby achieving a crystalline PGF without bond reversibility.

ideas.title: Biomimetic Pressure-Induced Crystallization for PGFs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that achieving crystalline order in porous glass films (PGFs) requires high-temperature or solvent-free synthesis. By drawing an analogy from biomineralization and protein crystallization, where high hydrostatic pressure (HHP) is used to induce high-quality crystal formation under mild aqueous conditions, we propose a biomimetic pressure-induced crystallization route for PGFs. The adapted research idea involves subjecting an aqueous precursor gel or colloidal suspension for PGFs to controlled, mild hydrostatic pressure (1-2 kbar) at near-ambient temperature. This physical force will compress the solvated building blocks, reduce system volume, and selectively stabilize the intermolecular interactions necessary for nucleation and growth of a crystalline framework. This method aims to achieve the desired long-range order and stability in PGFs while operating entirely under mild aqueous conditions, preserving framework porosity and avoiding the defects associated with thermal or solvent-free processing.

ideas.title: Apply Supramolecular Polymerization Pathways to Programmable Gel Frameworks
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from supramolecular chemistry, specifically pathway-controlled polymerization, to the METHOD_TRANSFER of reticular chemistry principles for building Programmable Gel Frameworks (PGFs). In supramolecular polymerization, the assembly pathway (kinetic vs. thermodynamic, seeded growth) is a critical design parameter for controlling the final polymer's structure and properties. This analogous solution is adapted by applying the principle of pathway control to PGF synthesis. Instead of using only isoreticular expansion for systematic building, the research would design gelation protocols that incorporate kinetic traps, catalytic initiation, or templated seeding to direct the hierarchical assembly of metal-organic or covalent gel components. This approach aims to create PGFs with precisely engineered architectures, gradients, or dynamic properties that are defined by their assembly history, expanding beyond static framework design.

ideas.title: Tuning PGF Properties via Controlled Skeletal Disruption, Inspired by Protein Engineering
ideas.description: The original assumption that a fully annulated aromatic skeleton is optimal for PGFs is challenged by drawing an analogy from protein engineering. In that field, the native, fully folded protein structure is not always ideal for function; flexibility is tuned by inserting linkers or making mutations. The analogous solution is to intentionally disrupt rigidity to achieve desired properties. Adapted to PGFs, this means designing materials where non-annulated or partially saturated linkers are not compromises but precise tools. By synthesizing a series of PGFs with strategically placed flexible tethers or saturated moieties, we can systematically tune the backbone flexibility, solubility, and solid-state packing. This approach moves beyond the rigid skeleton paradigm to create materials with optimized processability, charge transport, or mechanical properties for specific device applications.

ideas.title: Crystallization via pre-organized, photo-triggered irreversible coupling
ideas.description: This approach decouples the assembly and fixation steps to overcome the fundamental flaw of one-shot irreversible crystallization. Monomers capable of irreversible azide-alkyne cycloaddition are synthesized with a photoremovable group (e.g., o-nitrobenzyl) on the alkyne. These monomers are allowed to reversibly self-assemble into an ordered lattice through designed supramolecular interactions under conditions where the protected alkyne is inert. Once optimal order is achieved, uniform UV irradiation rapidly removes the protecting groups across the entire system, triggering simultaneous, irreversible covalent bond formation that captures and stabilizes the pre-existing crystalline lattice, yielding a crystalline polymer-grafted framework (PGF).

ideas.description: This study moves beyond basic selectivity screening to rigorously assess the practical viability of a new MOF for molecular separation. A custom test system will expose the material to cyclic stress conditions—such as pressure/vacuum swings, controlled humidity, trace contaminants, and temperature fluctuations—while continuously monitoring separation performance for target gas or liquid mixtures. In-situ characterization techniques will correlate any loss in selectivity or capacity with specific structural degradation or pore-blocking events. The outcome is a robust dataset predicting the MOF's operational lifetime and identifying failure modes under realistic process conditions.

ideas.title: Hierarchical PGFs via Vascular Network Templating
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from plant vascular systems, which efficiently solve multi-scale mass transport. The analogous solution from biomimetic engineering involves replicating hierarchical, interconnected pore networks through sequential templating and processing techniques. Adapted to PGF design, this involves a three-tiered fabrication strategy: 1) macroporous channels templated from a 3D-printed sacrificial scaffold for low-resistance bulk flow, 2) mesopores introduced via phase-separating templates or etchants within the framework walls for intermediate transport, and 3) intrinsic graphene micropores generated via controlled activation for final molecular access and adsorption. This biomimetic, hierarchical architecture aims to optimize mass transport across scales for applications like catalysis, energy storage, and advanced filtration.

ideas.title: Adapting Czochralski crystal growth for oriented PGF monolith synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Czochralski crystal growth method from semiconductor manufacturing to solve the scaling problem for PGFs (Porous Graphene Frameworks). In semiconductors, large, defect-free single-crystal boules of silicon are grown by pulling a seed crystal from a molten bath. The adapted research idea proposes using a similar pulling technique from a high-temperature precursor solution or melt to synthesize macroscopic, oriented PGF monoliths. By carefully controlling the withdrawal rate, temperature gradient, and atmosphere, this method could enable the production of large, single-crystalline PGF films or free-standing blocks with the structural perfection and orientation required for advanced electronic or photonic devices, directly addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension of the original seed.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided and Experimentally Validated Protein Linker Design for Robust Reticular Synthesis of PGFs
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fatal flaw of unpredictable protein linker behavior by embedding a rigorous protein engineering cycle within the reticular design process. First, computational protein design generates candidate linkers for isoreticular expansion, which are filtered using folding stability and binding interface predictions. Surviving candidates are encoded into genetically tunable libraries and subjected to high-throughput screening for assembly competence. Only linkers that pass this functional selection are used in the final framework synthesis, guaranteeing that the topological design principles of reticular chemistry are faithfully executed with biological building blocks.

ideas.title: Apply solid-state NMR with paramagnetic relaxation enhancement for probing metal centers in PGFs
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural biology—paramagnetic relaxation enhancement (PRE) in protein NMR—to the analysis of paramagnetic glass-formers (PGFs). In proteins, PRE uses paramagnetic metal ions to obtain long-range distance constraints and probe solvent accessibility. Adapted to PGFs, this involves using advanced solid-state NMR techniques to exploit the paramagnetism of intrinsic or doped metal centers (e.g., transition metals, lanthanides). The enhanced relaxation rates of nuclei near the paramagnetic center will be measured and analyzed to derive distance constraints, directly probing the local bonding environment, coordination geometry, and dynamics around the metal nodes in the disordered glassy matrix. This method provides a powerful way to extract structural information from PGFs where conventional NMR approaches are challenged by paramagnetic broadening and disorder.

ideas.title: Amorphous Metallic Glass Paradigm for Oriented Polymer Glasses
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that crystallinity is always required for superior performance in polymers by drawing an analogy from metallurgy. In that field, bulk metallic glasses (BMGs) demonstrated that amorphous atomic structures, achieved through rapid cooling and specific multi-component compositions, could exhibit mechanical properties surpassing those of crystalline metals. Adapting this solution, the research will explore if highly oriented but non-crystalline amorphous PGFs can match or exceed crystalline performance. This involves designing complex polymer compositions that resist crystallization during processing, while employing techniques to induce a high degree of molecular orientation within the amorphous glassy state. The objective is to create a new class of 'oriented polymeric glasses' where superior mechanical, thermal, or barrier performance is derived from anisotropic chain alignment and dense packing without long-range crystalline order.

ideas.title: Side-chain engineering on a fully annulated aromatic core to decouple electronic and mechanical properties
ideas.description: This approach retains a fully conjugated, planar, and annulated aromatic backbone as the electronically optimal skeleton for properties like charge transport. To tune material flexibility, solubility, and mechanical properties without degrading electronic performance, flexible alkyl, alkoxy, or other solubilizing side chains are introduced at strategic positions on the core periphery. This decoupling allows independent optimization: the core ensures high electronic performance, while the side chains govern processability, film morphology, and mechanical robustness. The system is tested by synthesizing a series with the same core but varying side-chain length and branching, then correlating structure with electronic metrics (mobility, bandgap) and mechanical properties (elastic modulus, crack onset strain).

ideas.title: Adapting Polymer Blend Morphology Analysis to Quantify In-Plane Order Effects on PGF Conductivity
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from polymer blend morphology studies to investigate the role of in-plane order on conductivity in PGFs. In polymer science, the impact of phase-separated domain morphology on bulk properties is understood by first characterizing the spatial architecture of domains and then modeling property pathways through that specific microstructure. We adapt this by proposing to map the nanoscale distribution of ordered regions within amorphous PGF films using conductive atomic force microscopy (cAFM) and peak force tunneling atomic force microscopy (PF-TUNA). This quantitative spatial data on domain size, shape, and connectivity will then feed into percolation theory models and finite element simulations of charge and ion transport. This approach moves beyond a simple crystalline-versus-amorphous comparison to directly correlate specific in-plane architectural motifs (e.g., percolating pathways of ordered regions, isolated islands) with measured electronic and ionic conductivity, providing a mechanistic, structure-property understanding of how local order influences long-range transport in these materials.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Prediction Methods for Polymorph Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical solution from computational protein structure prediction to the problem of predicting stable polymorphs of pharmaceuticaly relevant molecules (PGF). In protein folding, the field combines ab initio sampling, template-based modeling, and machine learning to efficiently navigate vast conformational spaces. Adapted to computational crystal structure prediction, the research would implement a multi-stage pipeline: initial broad sampling of crystal packing and molecular conformations using fast molecular dynamics and random search algorithms; intermediate filtering and modeling based on known analogous crystal structures; and final refinement/ranking using a machine learning interatomic potential trained on targeted DFT calculations. This hybrid approach aims to dramatically increase the efficiency and success rate of identifying synthesizable polymorphs before experimental work.

ideas.title: Quantifying the functional relationship between spatially resolved local order parameters and mixed conductivity
ideas.description: This study establishes a causal link between specific structural order and conductivity by moving beyond a simple binary comparison. A single material is progressively disordered in a controlled manner to create a continuous gradient of structural states. High-resolution spatial mapping of both local order parameters (e.g., correlation lengths, bond-angle distributions) and electronic/ionic conductivity is performed on identical sample regions. This approach directly reveals which quantitative metrics of in-plane order are the dominant physical drivers for each conduction mechanism, enabling predictive design of disordered materials for specific transport functions.

ideas.title: Defect-Corrected Macroscopic PGF Synthesis via In-Situ Diagnostics and Adaptive Growth Control
ideas.description: This approach focuses on achieving device-quality macroscopic PGF crystals by directly confronting the defect and strain problem inherent in scale-up. A synthesis platform is designed with integrated, real-time structural diagnostics that feed data into an adaptive control system. This system dynamically fine-tunes growth conditions and applies localized post-processing interventions during deposition to anneal defects and manage strain accumulation. The result is a robust fabrication process for large-area, oriented PGF films or monoliths with preserved and predictable functional properties suitable for demanding device applications.

ideas.title: Adapting Photolithographic Etching for Controlled Graphene Patterning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that bottom-up synthesis is the only viable path for creating ordered porous graphene frameworks by drawing an analogy from semiconductor microfabrication. In that field, photolithography uses a patterned photoresist mask to protect specific areas of a silicon wafer during etching, enabling precise top-down creation of complex microstructures. Adapted to graphene research, the solution involves first applying a molecular or polymer resist layer to the graphene sheet. This resist is then patterned with high precision using scanning probe lithography or electron-beam lithography. The patterned mask subsequently guides a controlled etching step (e.g., plasma or chemical etching), protecting specific graphene regions to create the desired porous framework with atomic precision. This approach merges top-down patterning with controlled material removal, offering a potential alternative pathway to bottom-up synthesis for creating ordered nanostructures in 2D materials.

ideas.title: Mapping the Continuous Structural-Property Landscape Beyond Crystalline and Amorphous Classifications
ideas.description: This research systematically explores the performance of polymer-grafted fibers (PGFs) across a continuous spectrum of structural order, moving beyond the crystalline/amorphous dichotomy. A combinatorial library is created by precisely varying processing parameters to generate gradients in orientational correlation, paracrystallinity, and medium-range order. High-throughput in-situ characterization and property testing, coupled with machine learning analysis, are used to identify optimal structural motifs—which may be hybrid or non-equilibrium states—for target properties like strength and thermal transport. The goal is to discover superior performing material states that would be missed by a binary comparative approach.
